The papyrus comes from the page of a book: it bears on the verso the remains of two lines of writing, and two designs in the form of foliage. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a private account
summary: Fragment of a private account. Perhaps this is part of the same document as 67219, but the two pieces do not join. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: List
summary: On the verso a list of  individuals, frequently qualified  with the name of a profession - stratiotes, chrysochoos, chalkotypos, bapheus. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a register
summary: Fragment of a register similar to those of comes Ammonios. Traces remain, unfortunately insignificant, of 17 pages folded one in another. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a private account
summary: Fragment of a private account, containing only one mutilated column of amounts. On the verso, some lines of figures. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Two fragments of an account
summary: Two fragments of an account, cut up irregularly by insects. The two pieces represent the remains of two consecutive sheets, and have identical shapes. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Five fragments of a private account
summary: Five fragments of a private account, which do not join. Also fragmentary remains of several columns, unfortunately not usable. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a private account
summary: Fragment of a private account, almost entirely corroded by the sebakh. Perhaps to be connected with 67214. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a private account
summary: Fragment of a private account. Similar hand to 67141 and 67142. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Payment of a loan with deposit
summary: From cartonnage found in Abusir el meleq. Hand similar to BGU IV 1155. On the recto contents of BGU IV 1134.

title: Fragment of a private account
summary: End of a sheet containing a private account. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a private account
summary: Fragment of a private account. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Receipt
summary: Tax receipt for 24 keratia.  Only the signature is completely preserved, which dates the document to the 19th of Hathyr and gives the name of Collouthos the hypodectes. It is followed by another receipt (15 keratia?) for the diagraphon. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Receipt
summary: Receipt relating to the annona militaris, issued to the protocometes of Aphrodito. This receipt is similar to those published above (67050, 67051, 67137). The formula used is the usual one for this type of document. On the verso, the beginning of the address is visible. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a receipt
summary: Fragment of a receipt similar to 67229. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a receipt
summary: Preceded by the number cmg. The text is, on the whole, damaged and has almost faded.  We may still make out the name of Phoibammon pronoetes in line 1. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Acknowledgement of a debt
summary: Acknowledgement of a debt and settlement of security with a creditor, by Dioscoros, son of Apollos. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragments of a private receipt
summary: Fragments of a private receipt (plerotike apoche). The receipt relates to the payment of certain dues (ekphoria). Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: Fragments of a contract (eggraphos homologia) drawn up by Kyros (nomikos). Signatures of witnesses, but only the name of Ioannes son of Charisios is preserved. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Report of Chaff Collectors
summary: A report of chaff and hay delivered by Seuthes and Isidorus, chaff collectors of Karanis in 310/311.

title: Conclusion of contract with a wet nurse; payment of a loan with paramon�
summary: From cartonnage found in Abusir el meleq. Top part is missing. Small cursive with many abbreviations. On the verso two documents, one under the other. On the recto, in a different hand, BGU IV 1156.

title: Fragments of a contract for the rent of land
summary: Fragment of a contract to rent land (misthosis).  There is a stipulation that fees must be in corn and in barley. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragments of a contract for the rent of land
summary: Fragment of land rental (note the word �permobaliva sic). Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract for the rent of land
summary: Fragment of a contract for the rental of land (misthosis).  Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract for the rent of land
summary: Fragment of a contract of misthosis. The contract specifies a period of rental of two years, of a piece of land situated in the kleros of Treges. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragments of a contract for the rent of land
summary: Fragment of a contract of misthosis. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a land rental contract (?)
summary: Note the expression idiois anal�masi te kai autourg�masi.  The papyrus has almost entirely faded. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a document
summary: The verso of the fragment is part of an unrecognizable document of which only a few words are preserved. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a deed of sale of land
summary: Fragment of a deed of sale of land. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract for the sale of land or a house
summary: Fragment of a contract for the sale of land or a house. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a private account
summary: On the verso, the remains of a private account. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Loan contract
summary: From cartonnage found in Abusir el meleq. Medium-sized hand, letters very close together, and therefore at times unclear. On the verso loan contract from 13 BC.

title: Two fragments of a document
summary: Two fragments, appearing to be parts of the same document: a deed similar to 67117 and 67119. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a rental deed
summary: Fragment of a rental deed, relating to a house (?). The writing, similar to that of  67159 and 67160, seems to indicate provenance from Antinoopolis. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Twelve fragments of a deed for a gift of land
summary: Twelve fragments, not  joining up, of a deed for the gift of land (the verb d�rein is repeated twice), in favour of a monastery, as the term to psuch�pheles, which is found on one of the pieces, seems to indicate. According to the beginning of the document, the period seems to be the reign of Justin II; a later date is not very probable. The town of Antinoopolis is mentioned; perhaps this is where it was drawn up. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: The end of a deposition
summary: End of a deposition (ekmarturion), c.f. 67087 and 67088. At the foot there is the signature by Fl. Paulos, followed, after a blank space, by the date. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Three fragments of a contract
summary: Three irregular fragments of a contract. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: A contract concerning land-rental(?) for a period of ten years. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragments of a rental contract
summary: Two fragments of a misthosis. Signed by Sansneus son of Isaakios, written by Abrahamios. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of an unspecified document (letter?)
summary: The document is very damaged.  Line 15 mentions the city of Alexandria. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a private account
summary: On the verso of the fragment are the traces of private account, and some lines written perpendicularly, which seem to be continuation of the recto. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: Fragment of a contract. Only the signature remains, preserving the name of Isaakios nomikos. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Loan contract
summary: From cartonnage found in Abusir el meleq. On the recto loan contract from 15 BC.

title: Fragment of a pittakion
summary: Fragment of a pittakion preserving the signatures of witnesses, Phoibammon and Aur. Papnouthios. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: A contract that is probably a misth�sis. Signatures of [�] son of Collouthos, in  the name of one of the contracting parties, and Pheib son of Isakios, who appears to have been a witness. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: Fragment of a contract, perhaps for a sale.  The words ebebai�sato and presbuterou can be made out. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: Fragment of a contract. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of an unrecognisable document
summary: The document is mutilated and half of it is faded. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: The contract contains the name Conon. The writing seems to be that of Dioscoros. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: Fragment of a contract. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Two fragments of an unspecified document
summary: The two fragments are of similar dimensions. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: Fragment of a contract  between a landowner and some nightwatchmen, who undertake to protect his property. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fourteen fragments of a register of accounts
summary: The fragments are very mutilated and unusable. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Loan contract
summary: From cartonnage found in Abusir el meleq. Large, squiggly hand. Occasional ommission of letters, not explicable as abbreviation. On the verso is BGU IV 1153.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: The contract is in coptic. The hand is similar to that of 67176, and this piece perhaps formed part of this document. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Grammatical exercise
summary: On the verso, some forms of the verb bo�n serving as an example for -a contracted verbs. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a document
summary: Fragment of papyrus almost entirely faded. The writing of Dioscoros? Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: The document is made of four lines crossed out by chiasmos. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment (anepigraphon)
summary: Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Petition
summary: Apollos, of the village of  Phoukis in the Antaiopolite nome, tenant farmer of the heirs of comes Phoibammon, complains of having been forced by the topot�r�t�s of Antinoopolis to pay taxes for which they should not be responsible. Written by Dioscoros; the fragment is missing a strip on the left, which had been found separately and published as 67010. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Poetry (Encomium)
summary: The poetry on the verso of this document is by Dioscoros. The poem represents an encomium on unnamed officials. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Order from the praeses of the Thebaid
summary: Order (prostagma) from the praeses of the Thebaid to the people of  Aphrodito, relating to the annona. This document is similar to 67030, but it carries a different date. Unfortunately the name of the praeses (komes kai archon) has disappeared, along with all the first line. Subscription on the verso, addressing the document to the people of Aphrodito. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Order
summary: Order (prostagma) of Dioscoros, praeses of Thebaid, to Apollos son of Isaac, charging him to accept the post of riparios of Aphrodito. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Copy of a commonitorium
summary: Duplicate of a notice (commonitorium) given by the offices of the praeses of  lower Thebaid to an unnamed official, perhaps the singularis Menas (topoteretes of Antaiopolis?).  The subject is an accused person who should be arrested and who the deliberate negligence of the people of the village has left at liberty. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Petition to the strategus
summary: From cartonnage found in Abusir el meleq. Clear cursive with occasional punctuation.

title: Report
summary: Report  (didaskalia) addressed to the Empress Theodora by the inhabitants of  Aphrodito, against the intrigues of the pagarch of Antaiopolis. The text seems to have been written by Dioscoros. The papyrus is made of three pages separated by ample margins;

title: Tax receipt
summary: Tax receipt issued to the inhabitants of Aphrodito, for the payment of 2 nomismata, allocated to the salary of the commentariensis (c.f. 67040). Subscription on the verso. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Documentary papyrus.
summary: A few lines from the end of an entagion, written along the fibres. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros. (In the Maspero edition this is referred to as verso).

title: Tax receipts
summary: Tax receipt, issued by Ioannes, hypodectes of Aphrodito, to the house of Dioscoros Psimanobet. The text is written on the verso of an older account, accross the fibres, in which a rectangular piece, almost regular, has been cut out. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros. (In the Maspero edition this is referred to as recto).

title: Two receipts for dues in kind
summary: Two receipts for dues in kind, due by the village of Aphrodito to the convent of the Penitence (Metanoia). Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Table of taxes and expenses
summary: Two fragments that do not join. Table of taxes and expenses of the town of Aphrodito, during the first indiction. The first column registers the sums spent for the kanonika and the military annona various expenses, notably salaries (synetheiai) of the officials; the following columns contain the enumeration of details of various expenses. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: List of taxpayers
summary: List of taxpayers emerging from the town of Aphrodito. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Private account
summary: Taxes due and expenses of Ioannes, a settler of a place called Ko[�] in the Lycopolite nome. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Administrative letter
summary: An employee of the office of the praeses(?) makes known to the protocometes Apollos that people in high places are unhappy with him, because he has not stopped a certain Eudoxios from abusing his power. The reconstructions of this incomplete piece are presented with all reservation. Address in one line on the verso. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Administrative letter
summary: This letter, addressed to Taurinos, singularis of the presidal office, by a superior employee of the same office. The village of Aphrodito should benefit from a tax exemption, which it has claimed. The end is unclear, due to missing parts of the text. The address on the verso of the document notes Taurinos, and his position as singularis. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Petition
summary: From cartonnage found in Abusir el meleq. Clear cursive, partly very abraded.

title: Fragment of an administrative letter
summary: The letter, of which this fragment is part, was addressed to Apollos, protocometes of Aphrodito; in all likelihood, a little of the text on either side is missing. On the verso, the letter is addressed to Apollos the protocometes by Ioannes, who is perhaps the pagarch of Antaiopolis who signed several of the receipts of 67325. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: It is possible that this letter was addressed to the protocometes of Aphrodito. The address on the verso is incomplete, and thus, any definitive answer regarding the addressees of this letter is tentative. However, the use of the term eudokimotatos seems to imply that the officials in question were protocometes (cf. 67291 verso). Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Duplicate of 67089
summary: Duplicate of 67089. Two groups of fragments, between which two lines are missing altogether. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Collection of four various parts
summary: This collection is the work of a copyist who was writing in the second half of the 6th century. This may explain the errors made by the scribe, errors which appear particularly numerous in document no. 2. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.
date_text: Second half VIth Century AD (Copy of col. I-II made towards 566-570)

title: Deed of surety
summary: Deed of surety, given to the riparios Apollos by the deacon  Anouphis, and an officer of the garrison of  Antaiopolis, on account of two workmen. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Deed of surety
summary: Deed of surety dated the same day as P.Cair.Masp. III.67296, but probably in a different hand. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Contract of emphyteusis
summary: Contract of  emphyteusis, between a certain Senouthes, clarissimus, and the church of Tentyra (Denderah).  However, it is more probable that the contract comes from Antinoopolis. Senouthes would be an inhabitant of this last town, and could have properties in distant nomes. The verso is entirely covered in tachygraphic signs, which are interrupted numerous times to give place to a few discontinued words. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Contract of emphyteusis
summary: The monastery of Saint Phoibammon sells to the lawyer  Phoibammon, on 'emphyteutic' lease, a cultivated field (psilon chorema, in contrast to built up land), with its dependencies, a cowshed and two kellia. The annual rent goes up to  19 keratia. On the erso, tachygraphic signs, similar to those which cover the verso of 67298. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Rental of land
summary: Sibylle and Herais, acting in the name of Sourous(?), have granted to Victor, son of Papnouthes, for a period of three years, a property situated in the kleros Ouesotos.  He undertakes to pay the taxes, in cash and in kind (chrysika, annona and naula), d to pay to the owners fee of 28 artabai of corn, 50 cheeses, four measures of vegetables in brine and a third of a golden coin. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Lease of a part of a house
summary: Lease of a part of a house by Aurelia Ama Rachel (?) to Isaac her brother. On the verso, text in tachygraphic characters. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Letter of Isidora to Asklepiades
summary: From cartonnage found in Abusir el meleq. The back is blank.

title: Lease of a wagon
summary: Lease of a wagon for the transport of a crop. The owner of the wagon is Dioscoros, son of Apollos, and the papyrus proves that he still lived at Aphrodito after the year 552. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: The contract refers to the employment of a steward. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Contract
summary: Contract of apprenticeship, and of board and lodging. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Reicept for a debt
summary: Receipt for a debt of 5 nomismata, and cancellation of the acknowledgement, which the creditor has not been able to find to return it to the debitor. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Receipt for dues in kind and money
summary: This receipt was paid by Apollos, son of Dioscoros, tenant of a piece of land belonging to the Church of Aphrodito. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Acknowledgement of a debt (asphaleia)
summary: Sansneous, son of Isac, has borrowed from Apollos two artabai of corn and two of barley, for the sowing of his field. The verso contains a subscription in a single line. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Contract of marriage
summary: This document is a contract of marriage between two unnamed individuals.  The form of the contract is somewhat irregular: it has been drawn up after the consummation of the marriage. The verso of the document seems to be a continuation of the marriage contract that appears on the recto of the document. Line 9, verso: suzugias sumph�non �toi gamikon sumbolaion. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Contract of Divorce
summary: The names of the two parties are lost: the clauses are similar to those in 67121, 67153, 67155. Dioscoros arrived at Antinoopolis in AD 566.  Following the restoration [trit�]s in line 1, the date is AD 569-70. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Will of Fl. Theodoros
summary: This document represents the will of Flavius Theodoros, employed in the offices of the dux of the Thebaid.  Cf. 67151, it is similar style, often the same phraseology, and has a long drawn out quality.  The document is incomplete. The testator, having no children, divides his fortune between three heirs: to the monastery of Saint Senouthios at Atripe, he leaves all his property located in the Hermopolite, Antinoite and Panopolite nomes or elsewhere, just as in the towns of Hermopolis and Antinoe, charging that the revenues from there be used for 'pious' works. To the convent of Apa Mousaios he leaves all his movable goods. To his grandmother he leaves an unspecified property. His slaves shall be freed. Two fragments, separated by a small lacuna (l. 78); the end is missing. The verso is occupied by a text in tachygraphic characters. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Division of inheritance
summary: Division of the inheritance of two brothers, Philemon and [...]. The first and only remaining part, consists of the terms on the part of Philemon: first, the field called  Apalasias, on the territory of the village of Arabon, with its dependencies and its islands; second, a house situated in this same town; third, two slaves, a brother and sister, and their future descendants. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Two letters of Isidora to Asklepiades
summary: From cartonnage found in Abusir el meleq. Three columns, the first only contains a few remains. The two complete columns contain the whole second letter.

title: Division of inheritance
summary: Division of an inheritance between a widow and her five sons. The five sons have inherited from their father, but are bound to certain obligations to their mother: g�roboskeisthai and nosokomeisthai in case of illness, (cf. 67097) paying the interest on a debt which she has, and lastly to pay her an income which the deceased has left (which is designated by the curious expression et�sion pekoulion. The hand is that of Dioscoros. The document is in three fragments, that do not join. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Contract of association
summary: This is the third example of a contract already known as 67158 and 67159. The hand is that of Dioscoros. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Poem (an Encomium)
summary: On the verso, a poem of Dioscoros to the dux of the Thebaid, Callinicus. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Protocol
summary: On the recto, five lines of large writing, reproducing a protocol similar to those which have appeared earlier, notably 67151. It is however less stylised, and therefore more legible, and would function as a valuable aid to the decipherment of such scripts, if only it were not in such a hopeless state of conservation. It consists of several small fragments, which could be re-arranged thanks to the verso. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Two poems by Dioscoros
summary: On the verso, two poems by Dioscoros: the first takes the subject of the death of Achilles (Dioscoros has adopted the legend according to which Achilles, lured into an ambush, would have been killed at the moment he celebrated his marriage with Polyxene) and is an ethopoieia; the second poem is addressed to comes Domninos cancellarius on the prefectural staff. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a poem by Dioscoros
summary: This poem, by Dioscoros, is in iambic trimetres.  The subject seems to be the praise of a dux of the Thebaid. Typical hand of Dioscoros. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Poetry
summary: Poem in Homeric hexametres, the work of Dioscoros: epithalamion of a certain Isaac (Isakios) (cf. 67179). Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: List of pastures
summary: List of pastures (bosk�mata) belonging to Dioscoros, son of Apollos, and that have been laid waste by his enemies. On the sense of the word bosk�ma, cf. 67097 (A).2; 67112.11.  As we already possess a list of the same nature, (67143) written in the hand of Dioscoros, it is possible that this also is concerned with him (although his name can be read with that of his grandfather Dioscoros Psimanobet). The attacks against his property would then be the result of the measures taken against him by the pagarch of Antaiopolis in the year 566 (67002) and which forced him to flee to Antinoe. This devastation no doubt consisted of having bestial strangers on his grazing land (cf. 67002). On the verso, there is a long subscription which has almost entirely faded. We may still read the first word and the last ones. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Order
summary: Order of the praeses of the lower Thebaid, Thomas, relating to the annona of the Garrison of  Antaiopolis, for the fifth indiction. The hand of this document is similar to that of 67030, 67280 and 67281. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Letter
summary: Letter of Ioannes (pagarch of Antaiopolis?) to a protocometes of Aphrodito. The subscription on the verso of the document is made up of two lines. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Property declarations
summary: Three property declarations addressed to the epimeletes. They give a brief specification of the property and an assessment of its value. All three are made out in the month Phaophi. [=Chrest.Wilck. 224a-c]

title: Edict of Aristius Optatus
summary: Aristius Optatus, a prefect of Egypt who is otherwise unknown, issued this edict on Phamenoth 20, to publicize and give effect to an edict previously issued by Diocletian in the name of the tetrarchs which introduced important changes in the tax system of Egypt.

title: Letter
summary: This letter is addressed to Apollos, a protocometes of Aphrodito, from Psaios, numerarius, employed in the offices of the praeses(?). Comes  Ammonios appeared already in 67062, 67138 and 67139. His title and his role here seem to prove that he was comes kai archon, that is to say, the praeses of the lower Thebaid. The subscription on the verso of the document notes both the name of the addressee, Apollos, and his position, as well as the sender, Psios. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: End of a will
summary: The end of the will of Aurelius Pankab, son of  Panouphis. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Inventory
summary: On the verso an inventory of movable property and furniture, in coptic. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Book of accounts
summary: The book is composed of eight leaves bound together by a thread, and contains documents of different sorts: the first relates to the administration of  an agricultural estate. The second, receipts for taxes (embole; demosia; naula) paid by a certain Cornelius, son of Philantinoos. The third, similar receipts, issued to Dioscoros. The fourth, a deed of rental of land belonging to a widow, daughter of Ioannes son of Cornelius. The script accounts appears to be that of Dioscoros; the tax receipts are in several other hands. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.
date_text: VIth Century AD (Fol. IV, verso Fol. II: AD 585; verso Fol IV: AD 585-86)

title: Receipt for rent
summary: The heirs of Kyros, represented by Christodoros, have received from Apollos, their tenant, the sum of the fees owed by him for the field situated at a place called  Piase, on the territory of the village of  Phthla. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Series of receipts
summary: Series of receipts similar to 67326, all issued to Apollos, son of Dioscoros by various landowners in the locality of  Piase. Belong to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Register
summary: Register containing a series of  guarantees (egguai), delivered to the riparios Apollos, and almost all dated the same day. This papyrus supplies us with fairly complete details on the role and the organization of the rural police of the poimenes kai agrophulakes. They must watch over the irrigation of the fields, generally attend to all sorts of public works not specified here, maintain order, seek out and make appear in court people who have been called to justice. Also worth noting is the division of the territory of the kome into decanies, each of which has its own guard. That of Teiae is cited twice. It has at least three poimenes; also that of Phenneus is named on two occasions. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Court proceedings
summary: The court proceedings of an interview given by the lawyer Fl. Paulos in the capacity of defensor civitatis of the city of Antaiopolis. The object of the debate is a transfer, by administrative means, of  the georgoi of the  village of Thmonechthe, or Thmonachthe, to that of Aphrodito, to cultivate there plots of public land. This transfer is referred to by the word metathesis. The people who take part in the debate are: Firstly, Psimanobet, peradjutor defensoris; secondly, Phoibammon, adjutor of the accounts office (logisterion) of the city of Antaiopolis; and thirdly, Apollos, protocometes of the village of Aphrodito. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Account of expenses
summary: Account of the expenditure of the village of Aphrodito; hand similar to 67058. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Remains of scholia on the Iliad
summary: Remains of scholia on the Iliad. Six fragments, being part of a codex. On the six fragments, two contain practically nothing, or contain only traces of writing insufficient to recognise to which part of the poem they relate. A third, no. 4, is more is more legible, but remains unidentified. The other fragments contain: Iliad, II, 414-421 (on the verso some indistinct words); Iliad, XVIII, 412-477 (verso: notes too damaged to be reconstituted; they relate to around verses 485-500); 552-603 on the verso, and on the recto some ends of lines. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Declaration of Olive Trees
summary: A declaration of twenty-four olive trees addressed to the censitor Julius Septimus Sabinus in accordance with an edict of the emperor Diocletian and his colleagues.

title: Fragment of a deed of surety
summary: Addressed to the protocometes. There are similar deeds that were presented to the pagarch (67094) and to the riparios (67328). Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: The damage to the text, as well as the obscure and incorrect jargon in which it is written, make it impossible to determine the nature of this document. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Deed of surety
summary: This document is fragment of a deed of surety.  Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragments of a receipt
summary: Fragments of a receipt  (plerotike apoche) issued to the protocometes of Aphrodito, for a tax. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Six fragments of a petition (?)
summary: The fragments are unfortunately not clear. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Iambic poem?
summary: pallatin�[-]. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a letter (?)
summary: The writing has almost entirely faded. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragments of a poem
summary: Fragments of a poem by Dioscoros, found mixed with those of 67337.  The encomium here also refers to the eponymous hero of Antaiopolis (Antaios had probably been identified with some form of ancient Egyptian divinity). Hand of Dioscoros. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Private account
summary: Private account. The hand is that of Dioscoros. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragments of a marriage contract
summary: The preserved part is quite long, but the roll of papyrus has been eaten away by insects, and large lacunae have appeared. The lines of writing are very spaced out on the recto, yet very close on the verso. This contract records that a woman is a widow or or divorcee, and there is already one son, whose fate is settled here. Proper names seem to have been carefully erased; however, as they are often repeated, a tentative reconstruction is possible: Akyllinos and Euprepeia. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Declaration of Land
summary: A declaration of land addressed to the censitor Julius Septimus Sabinus and submitted in accordance with the edict which authorized the census of 297 A.D.  The declarant is Herois, mother of Aurelius Isidorus, who here reports her holdings directly, and not through an agent as in Isid. 2.

title: Donation inter vivos
summary: Corrections have been added between the lines by a hand which could be that of Dioscoros. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Three leaves from a book of accounts
summary: Three leaves from an a book of  accounts, which may be of a similar kind and in the same hand as 67325. One of the leaves has on the verso traces of a contract, in which the name Pharoous still can be made out. Another, also on the verso, bears a first line, [. . . ]rogeuth( ) eis[ . . .]. The hand seems to be that of Dioscoros. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: The papyrus sheet initially contained a coptic text. Its verso was used for a letter addressed to a superior of a monastery(?). There is an address on the back of the letter (i.e. the recto of the sheet, which also contains the remains of coptic). Belong to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: Fragment of a contract relating to a loan (1 nomisma less 2 keratia). Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a tax receipt
summary: This fragment is similar to 67041. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Letter
summary: This document is in several fragments. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Remains of an account book
summary: An account book, relating to the fortune of comes Ammonios (cf. 67138 and 67139; also same hand). Fragments of six very mutilated leaves remain. Found mixed up and crumpled, these leaves could not be replaced in their original order. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: The hand is that of Dioscoros. However, the ink seems to have disappeared almost everywhere. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: (A) The sale, to a monastery, of the ktema called Pauonmeio. (B)The fragment may be another part of the same fragment of A. (C) A similar fragment to both 67350A and 67350B.  However, little can be drawn from this fragment. The hand is that of Dioscoros. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Grammatical exercise
summary: (A) On the verso of this document, part of the conjugation of the verb poiein (in three columns). (B) On the verso, part of the conjugation of the verb bo�n, and that of poiein. (C) On the verso, vague traces of a conjugation (poiein).

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: Small fragment of a contract, written in coptic.  This papyrus is probably a fragment of  67176: although the colouring of the papyrus differs, this could be explained by differing findspots. The handwriting on the recto and verso are the same in the two papyri (cf. 67275). Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Grammatical exercise
summary: On the verso some lines of the conjugation of the verb  poiein.

title: Fragment of a draft of a petition
summary: Fragment of a draft of a petition to the emperor. The surface of the papyrus has disappeared in many places, and consequently, reconstruction of what remains is impossible. Anonymous complaint against a man who has deprived him of his fortune. Probably the hand is that of Dioscoros. The text continues on the verso. It seems that the applicant asks for a stipend taken from the annona of the Hermopolite nome. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Contract in Coptic
summary: Long contract in Coptic, unfortunately fragmentary. The roll has crumbled and rotted in the sebakh, in such a way that the papyrus has taken almost everywhere a darkened colouring, which makes it difficult to read. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Greek text
summary: Several texts in Greek, written by Dioscoros, very damaged. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Two fragments of a petition
summary: Two fragments of a request  to the empress Theodora (cf. 67283). Line 6 indicates that it concerns a request to the empress, who is patroness of the village of Aphrodito. Written by Dioscoros.  The two fragments do not join. Very bad state of conservation; the writing is largely rubbed off. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Two fragments of a book of accounts
summary: Two fragments of a book of accounts. The two fragments appear to be of two consecutive pages, and the insects have eaten them irregularly, in an almost identical way. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: Fragment of a letter. Its height is complete. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Several fragments of a deposition
summary: Several fragments of a deposition (ekmartyrion), cf. 67087 and 67088. Belongs to the archive of Dioscoros.

title: Fragments
summary: Under this number are grouped the scanty remains of a papyrus from which nothing can be extracted.

title: Land Register
summary: A list of the landholders of Karanis and their properties.

title: Account relating to the impositions upon Antinoe
summary: Account relating to the impositions upon Antinoe. Five pieces sold separately to the museum, but evidently having formed a notebook of at least eight sheets whose order has been restored. Four (or more) pages are missing between the surviving folios numbe

title: Petition to the riparii
summary: Cursive, written across the fibres. Preserved in two copies, the other is P.Cair.Preis.03. Aurelios Serenos complains that his mother-in-law gave his wife away to another man.

title: Petition to the riparii
summary: Duplicate of P.Cair.Preis.02, written in the same cursive hand, along the fibres.

title: Legal complaint
summary: Large uncial cursive, running across the fibres. The papyrus is written on both sides. On the recto there are records of farming activities. The verso, which preserves parts of three columns, contains copies of correspondence etc. of Adelphios, a bouleutes of Hermoupolis.

title: Records
summary: The papyrus is written on both sides. On the recto there are records of farming activities.

title: Official business
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres.

title: Medical report
summary: Aurelius Ammonius, the public doctor, sends this report to the ekdikos.

title: Report of land measurement
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. The report is sent to the strategos or exactor by the 'public land-measurer' and the town gnoster.

title: Excerpt from Declarations of Land
summary: A summary of declarations made, probably in 298 or 299, by Heracles and Alexander, sons of Horion, for the census conducted in the Arsinoite nome by Julius Septimus Sabinus.

title: Account concerning Idios Logos
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. The strategoi are delegating work concerning the Idios Logos.

title: Petition
summary: Practiced cursive, written along the fibres. The back is blank. The petition probably has to do with the epicrisis of a boy. The papyrus is broken at the top and the foot.

title: Extract from a list of collectors
Building permissions
summary: Uncial-cursive, written along the fibres. There is a margin on the left and at the foot. The fragment seems to be part of a list of the overseers of the Arsinoite Nome concerning building works in three villages.

title: Letter offering surety
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. P.Cair.Preis.14 is its duplicate, but written in a different hand.

title: Letter offering surety
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. P.Cair.Preis.13 is its duplicate, but written in a different hand.

title: Letter offering surety
summary: Uncial-cursive, written along the fibres.

title: Letter offering surety
summary: Cursive, same hand as P.Cair.Preis.17, which is its duplicate.

title: Letter offering surety
summary: Cursive, same hand as P.Cair.Preis.16, which is its duplicate.

title: List of suggestions for liturgies
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. The same hand as P.Cair.Preis.19, which is its duplicate. The document comes from the village of Prektis.

title: Declaration of Persons
summary: A report of persons, addressed to Ulpius Alexander, censitor of the Heptanomia.

title: List of suggestions for liturgies
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. The same hand as P.Cair.Preis.18, which is its duplicate. The document comes from the village of Prektis.

title: List of liturgies
summary: A list of persons charged with liturgies and the inhabitants of an amphodon in Hermoupolis. The nature of office held is not stated. The list was for the use of the amphodarches. Five different hands can be discerned in this fragment.

title: Certificate of dam works
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. Preisigke explains that the more general parts of this sort of certificate must have been pre-written and kept in supply, while the detail would have been addded by an overseer and signed by a high-up official.

title: Storage account
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. Nine different hands can be discerned in the three columns that remain. The grain storage referred to in the document is either the local storage in Hermoupolis, or the one in Alexandria.

title: List of official acquisitions
summary: The text runs on both sides. The right margin of the front is actually the right margin of the entire roll. The front is actually the palaeographic verso. The list covers the period from Pharmouthi 23 to Pachon 3 of an unknown year.

title: A notary's register of contracts
summary: Small, gracefully executed uncial script, written along the fibres. Only five fragments remain from what was once a long roll. The preserved text contains parts of twenty-one excerpts of contracts, most being lease contracts, but there are also a few contracts of service.

title: Receipt of paid maritime freight
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. The receipt occupies only the upper half of the sheet, the lower half is blank.

title: Report of Sitologoi
summary: A statement addressed to the praepositus of the fifth pagus of the Arsinoite nome by the six sitologoi of the village of Karanis and its horiodeikti; a report of total amount of their collections of wheat, barley and lentils, and itemized record of same.

title: Certificate of maritime freight
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. As in a few other similar certificate documents, there is no guarantee for the safety of the freight.

title: Receipt of interest
summary: The writing runs along the lines; the back is blank.

title: Receipt of payment
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres.

title: Conract for the sale of cattle
summary: Uncial hand, written across the fibres. A cattleherd, whose name is partly lost, is buying 3 oxen.

title: Lease of land
summary: Uncial cursive, written along the fibres. The whole document  is written in one hand only. The lessor bids 1/2 and 1/3 artaba interest for each aroura, but with the addition that, in case someone else should offer a higher interest rate, he should also pay this higher rate.

title: Offer to lease land
summary: Very practiced cursive hand, written along the fibres. Flavius Cassius offers to lease fifteen arouras from the cleros of Aurelia Charite.

title: Offer to lease land
summary: Uncial-cursive, written along the fibres. Aurelius Silvanus offers to lease a plot of land belonging to Aurelius Adelphius also called Dionysodorus.

title: Lease of land
summary: Thick uncial cursive, written along the fibres.

title: Will
summary: Uncials, clearly written, running along the fibres.

title: Account
summary: Practiced cursive, written along the fibres. The back is blank.

title: Account of Chaff Collections
summary: A daily record of chaff collected as taxes on the harvest of 310/311 A.D.  The names of the collectors are given in the second line of the text as Isidorus and Heras.

title: Account of payments
summary: Uncials written along the fibres.

title: Land - survey list
summary: Uncials written along the fibres. The list differenciates between private and public land distributed according to pagoi. At the foot, in a different hand, is the incomplete signature of Asclepiades.

title: Land - survey list
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. Two columns. The papyrus breaks at top, foot and on the left. On the right the margin is preserved.

title: Private letter
summary: Cursive, written along the fibres. On the back there is the address. Dionysios writes to Heracleides, who is looking for a boat.

title: Loan of Money
summary: A contract, of a common type, by which Dionysios, a native of Gaza in Palestine, lends 34 drachmae to a Thracian cleruch called Isidorus.  This sum is to be repaid in three months' time, after the harvest.

title: Letter from Apollonius to Zenon
summary: A letter of introduction, requesting Zenon to give Nikomachos and Zoilos a berth on one of the coasting vessels and to assign them the usual salary.

title: Sale of a girl slave
summary: A duplicate deed of sale, attested by six witnesses, of a little girl called Sphragis, perhaps a Babylonian.  The seller was Nikanor, the buyer Zenon, and the price paid was fifty drachmae.  The sale took place in the land of Ammon, in a place called Birta. Several of the persons mentioned in the text are said to be cleruchs belonging to the troops of Toubias, an Ammonite chief of the great Toubiad family.

title: A flour account
summary: An account of the quantities of flour received and used by a party travelling in Palestine.  The first column gives a list of eleven towns in which flour was obtained.  The second column, and the two columns of the verso, state what quantities were given out from time to time to bakers amd other persons in a period of about four months, from Audnaios to Xandikos.

title: Fragment of an account
summary: A fragment of an account, written in the same hand as PCZ. 59004

title: Account
summary: A list of persons to whom pickled fish had been sold or given out from the store, somewhere in Palestine and probably not far from Gaza.  From the titles borne by several of these persons, it may be inferred that most of them belonged to a military garrison stationed in these parts.

title: Report of Sitologoi
summary: A summary report of the collection and subsequent distribution of taxes in wheat and barley by the sitologoi of Karanis and its horiodeiktia for the year 308/309, submitted at the request of the ex-praepositus Aurelius Heracles.

title: Fragments of an account
summary: Two of several decayed and discoloured fragments of a long account stating the quantities of wine given out day by day to certain persons.

title: Account
summary: An account of the quantities of bran given out for the use of mules, camels and donkeys in Palestine, in the autumn of a certain year, probably 259 B.C.

title: Fragments
summary: Nine fragments of a trading account or accounts, resembling PCZ.59008 in hand-writing and appearance.  On the verso of a: and b: are some fragmentary remains of a later account in a different hand, mentioning Komoapis, Dionysios, Orseus (?), Herakleides and the months Mesore, Thoth, Phaophi.

title: Account
summary: An account of the money handled and used by a commercial agent, who had been travelling between Alexandria and the Syrian coast, visiting the towns of Sidon and Askalon.

title: Fragments of a series of letters
summary: Fragments of a series of letters, which may be copies or carefully prepared drafts.  Those on the recto refer to the Palestinian period of Zenon's career.  The second letter in column 1 speaks of Bethanath and Joppa, while the first in column 2 seems to be about a consignment of frankincense and myrrh. Those on the verso mention Philadelphia, and the second letter is perhaps concerned with the work of the quarrymen there.

title: A valuation of imported goods
summary: A detailed valuation of a consignment of goods which had passed through the Customs at Pelusium on their way to Alexandria.  They were carried on two vessels commanded by Patron and Herakleides.  The list is divided into four main sections; three are the importers or consignees, and the fourth is the property of the ship or crew. Within these sections the goods are classed into four grades; those which paid 50% import duty, and those which paid respectively 33.3%, 25% and 20%. In each of these grades again they are subdivided into the articles carried by Patron and those carried by Herakleides.

title: Porterage account
summary: The articles mentioned in this account, including the marginal list, comprise practically all the goods imported by Herakleides for Apollonios with a few additional things.  The phoretron was probably the charge for porterage between the Mareotic harbour and the store-house in Alexandria rather than that at the ports where the cargo was picked up. The few objects not mentioned in the tim�sis appear to have paid no duty at the Customs; but several of them may have counted as ship's furniture, while the others were of small value. Crosses opposite the entries in the left margin of the main account show that the list has been checked over.

title: Porterage account
summary: The porterage account for Apollonios' share of the cargo of Patron.  Many of the goods can easily be identified with those entered in the tim�sis.  The articles of which there is no mention in the latter, lampstands, beds, table, sideboard, etc., were either not subject to import duty or were admitted free because they counted as ship's furniture.

title: Account concerning a consignment of oil
summary: The importer seems to have been Apollonios and the account seems to have been drawn up in his office.  Its object is to show the value, or nominal value, of the shipment after payment of Customs duty, freight and minor taxes.  Some marginal notes have been added in a smaller hand, whether by Zenon or by an accountant. The fact that the oil was carried in Milesian and Samian jars indicates its origin, and a comparison of the account with PCZ.59012 leads us to believe that it came by sea to Alexandria and was unloaded at that sea-port.

title: Letter from Demetrios to Zenon
summary: Demetrios, a grammateus stationed in Cyprus, had gone over to Phoenicia on some business, and having expended his travelling allowance on purchases in Tyre he begs Zenon to send him 150 drachmae to Beirut.  Zenon himself at this time was probably leading a wandering life, as Demetrios asks him where he will have to send him the money.

title: Loan contract
summary: From cartonnage. Cursive hand, same as BGU IV 1055, BGU IV 1058. Consists of two sheets stuck together. The other side (front) only carries writing on the first page.

title: Assignments of Epinemesis
summary: A complete roster of those landholders at Karanis to whom parcels of land were assigned for cultivation at Ptolemais, Bacchias, and Kerkesoucha Agoras in 313/314 A.D.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Letter from Alexandros (?) to Oryas
summary: Zenon had written to Oryas to recover some money from a Jew called Jeddous, or else to seize securities for it.  Oryas sent on Zenon's messenger, a man called Straton, with a copy of the letter, to Alexandros.  The latter having taken a dose of medicine was unable to leave his house (or perhaps he did not relish the task), but he sent a youth along with Straton to give a note to Jeddous and to recover the money. The two returned empty-handed, reporting that Jeddous had paid no attention to the note, but had assaulted them and driven them out of the village. I have therefore reported to you, says Alexandros, declining further reponsibility.

title: Letter to Zenon
summary: The loquacious writer, whose name is lost, speaks in the first part of the letter about an expert who had been called in to cure dice made of gazelles' bones and who according to his own story had been employed by members of the Court, including Antipatros the Etesian. In the second part he tells about a dispute between Patron the captain of a merchant vessel and a trader or commercial agent called Apollophanes. The letter was probably written in Alexandria, shortly after Zenon had started on a voyage, and it evidently belongs to the early years of the correspondence.

title: Fragment of a letter to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter to Zenon.

title: Letter from Demetrios to Apollonios
summary: Demetrios, who was probably head of the Alexandrian mint, informs Apollonios that following his instructions he has received a certain quantity of gold and converted it into new coin.  The gold was brought to him from abroad in accordance with a decree.

title: A money account
summary: A detailed account of a sum of money owned by the writer, or rather by his employer, the total being 2 talents, 2,124 drachmae.  Of this sum 2 talents in gold of various denominations had been deposited in the bank of Stratokles.  The gold coins did not exactly make up the amount of 2 talents; there remained a balance of 10 drachmae 4 obols in favour of the depositor, and this he had drawn out of the bank. Below is a line in smaller hand-writing, evidently a correction of lines 10-20. The gist of it is that the gold deposited in the bank was less by one octadrachm than the amount mentioned above, so that, instead of 1-drachmae 4 obols having been withdrawn, a sum of 93 drachmae 2 obols had been paid in to complete the two talents.

title: Fragment from a series of letters
summary: The only draft preserved entire in this series is that of a letter addressed to Stratokles, the banker mentioned in PCZ.59022, telling him to make up his acount and give it along with the balance to Iatrokles, one of Apollonios' employees.

title: Letter from Kriton to Zenon
summary: Kriton, the admiral of Apollonios' little fleet, asks Zenon to redeem a pledge for an absconding sailor and send him back to his boat.
date_text: Date of reception: About 29th October, 258 BC (?)

title: Letter from Archelaos to Kriton
summary: Archelaos, who was starting on a voyage, asks Kriton the admiral of Apollonios' fleet, to buy him two spars and a boat with three sculls and to look after his family in his absence.

title: Two fragments of a letter to Zenon
summary: In the first fragment the writer speaks of sending gifts of food to Apollonios and Zenon; in the second he asks Zenon to speak to Zoilos, the Arsinoite oikonomos, on his behalf.

title: Report of Chaff Collectors
summary: A report submitted to an official of the Heptanomia in the summer of 314 A.D. by Seuthes, son of Heras, and Isidorus, son of Ptolemaeus, chaff collectors of Karanis and its horiodeiktia for the year 310/311 A.D.

title: Letter from Aristeus to Apollonios
summary: Aristeus, an employee in the household of Apollonios, informs his master that, by order of Amyntas, he has paid the wages of the servants left behind in Alexandria.  A clay sealing was found loose inside this letter.  It bore a representation of Athena Promachos to right, with shield and spear.

title: Letter from Satyra to Zenon
summary: The girl called Satyra was harper in the Alexandrian household of Apollonios.  In the present letter, which is more emphatic than grammatical, she complains on behalf of herself and her mother that they have received  no dress allowance for more than a year and very little in the way of salary. In two other papyri (PCZ 59059 and 59087), we hear of her receiving ops�nion and linen garments.

title: Letter from Antimenes to Zenon
summary: Zenon came from Kaunos in Caria and had many friends and relations living there.  Though he himself was too busy to visit his native country during his service under Apollonios, other members of the family occasionally made the voyage from Caria to Egypt, or from Egypt to Caria. This letter from Zenon's friend Antimenes sends him word from Caria about the voyage of a lady in whom he evidently took an affectionate interest. Whether she was sailing to Egypt or elsewhere is not quite clear, but we may suppose that she embarked at Kaunos.

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: A letter concerning the hipparia which he had been ordered to send from Alexandria, probably required by Apollonios and his party for inland excursions.

title: Letter from Poseidonios to Apollonios
summary: The writer of this letter was a member of the Alexandrian court, holding the title of 'king's taster', perhaps the father of Arsinoe who had been eponymous priestess in year 26.  He complains that the employees of Sosistratos, the head of the toll station at Memphis had confiscated and sold some iron which they had found on one of his boats, whereas according to Poseidonios the iron was really a necessary part of the boat's equipment.

title: Letter from Amyntas (?) to Zenon
summary: Amyntas (?) asks Zenon to look after a certain person, whose name is lost or imperfectly preserved.

title: Letter from Nikias to Apollonios
summary: Apollonios had written to a certain Lysimachos, evidently a man of high rank, asking him to give his messengers some fruit trees for his or their plantations.  As Lysimachos happened to have gone down to Alexandria, the letter was answered by his agent Nikias, who writes that he has shown the messengers round all the orchards and advised them to send a delegate to Lysimachos, who could give them a better supply from down the river. The messengers, however, preferred to take what they could get from Nikias, and he adds a list of the sorts which they actually took: fig-trees of six kinds, pomegranate, apricot, apple and eleven varieties of vine.

title: Petition from Zoilos to Apollonios
summary: Zoilos, who resided abroad, had been ordered by the god Sarapis to sail over to Alexandria and inform Apollonios of his decree that a temple should be built for him in the Greek quarter of the town where the writer lived.  Seeking to decline the task, Zoilos was overtaken by a dangerous illness, from which he escaped by promising to obey the god's bidding. Meanwhile a man from Knidos had arrived and started to build a Sarapieion on the chosen spot; but the god forbade him and he departed. When Zoilos eventually came to Alexandria, instead of communicating the message, he spoke to Apollonios about another affair, with the result that he had a long relapse. He now writes to Apollonios, who was travelling in the interior, relating the whole story and begging him to provide means for building the temple.

title: Letter from Statios (?) to Zenon
summary: A badly preserved letter, the meaning of which is not quite clear.  The writer asks Zenon to give some money to Zenodoros in order that he may buy him a sword-belt, and he says that he has written to Zenodoros to carry out the order.

title: Letter from Apollodotos to Charmides
summary: Apollodotos was a subordinate of Apollonios the dioik�t�s, possibly a royal oikonomos, residing in Caria.  Charmides to who he writes was an agent of his, probably staying Alexandria.  He sends Charmides, along with his instructions, copies of two letters addressed to a certain Xanthippos. We learn from the text that Xanthippos was trierarch of a ship and we gather from the context that he was a trierarch in the Athenian sense; i.e. a rich man on whom was laid the burden of equipping a ship for the Ptolemaic navy. While he himself was inhabiting or visiting Alexandria, the ship was stationed at Halikarnassos under the charge of his representative Antipatros. The latter being short of money for upkeep or repairs had received, through the influence of Apollodotos, the following advances, or friendly loans without interest out of certain public funds.

title: Account of Taxes in Kind
summary: A day book of tax contributions in grain made by landholders of Karanis.

title: Letter or memorandum to Zenon
summary: A letter probably from one of Zenon's Carian friends, in which he informs Apollonios, through Zenon, that letters had been sent from Apollodotos and Kratinos, son of Danaos, proposing that the affair of Danaos should be settled by the payment of twenty talents to the Crown, but that the bearer of the letters, instead of delivering them, was wasting his time in Alexandria in the house of Aristoboulos, He also reports that Danaos' friend Pankris was trying to obtain the office of nomophylax through Epikydes and that the question was being referred to Apollonios; and he advises the latter to show no favour to a man who was talking scandal about him. He next asks for letters of recommendation from Apollonios to Apollodotos, Laagos and Hikesios and for another letter to Iason the banker certifying that Apollonios had been appeased through the writer's intercession.

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: Amyntas writes about his friend Demetrios, to who he had lately lent some silver plate belonging to Apollonios, and who in the meantime had been arrested.  If Demetrios' goods are being confiscated by the Treasury, Zenon must try to recover the plate; if not, he is requested to let Demetrios keep it. Amyntas also asks him to show Demetrios as much consideration as possible, until he himself finds an opportunity of interceding with Apollonios. (A fuller account of this affair appears in PCZ.59044.)

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Amyntas to Zenon, which came in the same post as PCZ.59038, about a certain Molossos, whom Amyntas had recommended to Apollonios.

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: Amyntas ackowledges receipt of the copper vessels from the Troglodyte and of the fish, all but two, from Aigyptos.

title: Letter to Zenon
summary: This incomplete and rather obscure letter is about a tax-collector who had been arrested and taken to the Arsinoite nome as though responsible to the authorities there.  The writer was probably a tax-farmer at Memphis or in the Memphite nome, and the arrested man had been an employee of his and was now an employee of his colleague or successor Athenodoros. Apollonios had already ordered Theodoros to write to Zoilos, the Arsinoite oikonomos, and to Mnesistratos to return the man to his employers, and the writer asks Zenon to procure Apollonios' order and send it on to him.

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: The docket tells us that the travellers were now in Boubastos, no doubt the Metropolis in the Delta.  Amyntas recommends a friend of his called Alexandros, who had been requisitioned for some public service, and asks Zenon to get him excused on the plea of ill health.
date_text: Date of reception: About 19th March, 257 BC

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: Amyntas reports that the servants in Alexandria are clamouring for their wages and asks Zenon to give instructions, as Apollonios has no time to think about such things.
date_text: Date of reception: About 24th March, 257 BC

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: Amyntas writes about his friend Demetrios, whose goods were now being sold up and who was himself still in prison.  He offers proof that the silver plate in question really belongs to the household.  Subjoined is a copy of a letter which he has written to Apollonios, but which he does not wish Zenon to deliver unless he and Artemidoros the physician agree that it is advisable. The object of the letter to Apollonios is not to plead for Demetrios, but to excuse himself for having lent Demetrios the plate in the beginning.
date_text: Date of reception: About 26th March, 257 BC

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: This letter introduces Zopyros the son of Sosigenes, who, like Zenon, belonged to a Kaunian family, and requests Zenon to choose a favourable moment for presenting him to Apollonios, to whom he wished to speak about some private matters.  Amyntas had also written a letter of recommendation to Apollonios (PCZ.59046), which would probably be delivered by Zenon or by Zopyros himself.
date_text: Date of reception: About 26th March, 257 BC

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: A letter of recommendation, in which Amyntas asks Apollonios to give Menandros a favourable hearing.  For, he adds,  he is the sort of man that will care for your interests.

title: List of Donkey Caravans
summary: A list of caravans used for transporting grain from Karanis to the Nile ports.  In its present state, the papyrus provides information concerning 32 trips made under the supervision of nine men.

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: Amyntas sends Dexilaos with a letter to Apollonios about expenses and asks Zenon to deliver the letter and send back the messenger at once, presumably with money or with an order on the bank.  Inside the letter was found a clay sealing with an indistinct representation of a man to right, wearing a conical hat.

title: Memorandum to Aratos from Aristeus
summary: Aristeus, a man of good position in the household of Apollonios, asks Aratos to remind Zenon the oikonomos and Kriton the admiral to purchase some rugs and garments and a Tanitic bed-spread and bring them down to Alexandria, as he had asked them to do before they left town.

title: Letter from Nikon to Zenon
summary: In the first part of the letter Nikon asks Zenon to send a man, or allow him to hire a man, who will take delivery of the hay due to the estate from certain farmers.  The second part is a personal appeal for better pay.

title: Fragment of a letter to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter.  It is not clear whether the writer was Amyntas or some other person.

title: Fragment of a letter from Poseidonios
summary: A fragment of a letter from Poseidonios about three artabs of wheat.
date_text: Date of reception: About 14th April, 257 BC

title: Letter from Antimenes to Zenon
summary: Probably a continuation of PCZ.59029, in which Antimenes announced the departure of a lady, probably from Kaunos or some other Carian port.  The docket on the verso states that Antimenes has written about Doris and has added a copy of a letter received by him from Sosipatros. He now announces that Ariston and the sister, no doubt Doris, have arrived at Arsinoe after being compelled by stormy weather to put in at Patara, and he thanks Antimenes for the attention he has paid to the travellers.
date_text: Date of reception: About 20th April, 257 BC

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: Amyntas has learned that he will soon be ordered to go on a voyage, and he asks Zenon to have awnings and other things made for his boats and to bring them down with him when he returns to Alexandria.  He has sent Hermon to give a list of the things requirequired and to attend to the business of procuring them.

title: List of articles required for a voyage
summary: As this papyrus was found sticking to PCZ.59053, there is no doubt that it is the list of which Amyntas speaks at the end of that letter.  He was apparently to have the use of two boats, a kerkouros and a kubaia and for each boat he required a set of four ears that the two boats were of equal length, but the kubaia was broader throughout and blunter at the bow. Column 2 contains a list of miscellaneous articles, including a hundred and fifty papyrus rolls for accounts and correspondence.

title: Fragment of a letter from Poseidonios
summary: A fragment of a letter from Poseidonios, concerning possibly an auction of pigs.
date_text: Date of reception: end of April, 257 BC

title: Letter from Apollodotos to Zenon
summary: An incomplete letter written in Caria by Apollodotos, who had lately gone to Kaunos and met Zenon's father and brothers, and he writes to let Zenon know that he has been showing them every attention and will be pleased to do anything else that they or Zenon may desire. He wonders why he has not heard from Zenon for such a long time.
date_text: Date of reception: About 27th April, BC

title: List of Landholders
summary: A list of personal names followed by a statement of his or her (and in one case a husband and wife together) owned or leased area expressed in arouras.

title: Letter from Zoilos to Alexandros
summary: Zoilos (probably employed by Apollonios in Syria and Palestine) urges Alexandros to help him and bring pressure to bear on Apollonios by means of his friends, but he does not say what exactly he is wanting.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: The meaning of this letter, owing to the state of its preservation, is obscure.  From Apollonios to his faithful secretary Zenon.
date_text: Date of reception: About 28th April, 257 BC

title: Letter from Aristeus to Zenon
summary: Aristeus announces that he will pay the servants in Alexandria according to Zenon's instructions and gives some details about previous payments.
date_text: Date of reception: About 5th May, 257 BC

title: Letter from Hierokles to Zenon
summary: This letter is about a boy called Pyrrhos, who was being educated in Alexandria and was in particular being trained to compete in the public games.  Zenon had written that they might train him if they were sure that he would win a prize, but that otherwise it would be a waste of money and would distract him from his studies. Hierokles replies that Ptolemaios the master of the palaestra is well pleased with the boy's progress and that they fully expect him to be successful. He asks Zenon to send him a number of articles required by Pyrrhos.
date_text: Date of reception: About 5th May, 257 BC

title: Letter from Hierokles to Zenon
summary: The is letter is almost a duplicate of the second part of PCZ.59060, about a boy called Pyrrhos, who was being educated in Alexandria and was in particular being trained to compete in the public games.  Zenon had written that they might train him if they might train him if they were sure that he would win a prize, but that otherwise it would be a waste of money and would distract him from his studies. Hierokles replies that Ptolemaios the master of the palaestra is well pleased with the boy's progress and that they fully expect him to be successful. He asks Zenon to send him a number of articles required by Pyrrhos.
date_text: Date of reception: About 4th May, 257 BC

title: Letter from Theodoros to Zenon
summary: Theodoros, a brother of Amyntas, writes to say that, having found Python, the well-known banker, at Athribis, he had received from him the sum of one thousand drachmae.  He also asks Zenon to deliver, at a propitious moment, a letter from Amyntas to Apollonios about Python and to see that an order is written about the payment of his salary.
date_text: Date of reception: About 5th May, 257 BC

title: Fragment of a letter from Metrodoros to Apollonios
summary: A fragment of a letter in which Metrodoros announces the dispatch of three Kythnian cheeses for Apollonios' table.
date_text: Date of reception: About 7th May, 257 BC

title: Fragment of a letter from Metrodoros to Apollonios
summary: A fragment of a letter from Metrodoros to Apollonios.

title: Fragment of a letter to Apollonios
summary: A fragment of a letter to Apollonios, possibly from Metrodoros, in which he mentions sending Apollonios a consignment of thrissai by Apollonios the herm�neus.

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: Amyntas announces that he has sent Apollonios a consignment of fish of various kinds and regrets that he has been unable to send Zenon the Chian wine and the other things which he had ordered.  The excuse he gives is that the king is summoning Apollonios

title: List of Metropolitans
summary: A list of citizens of Arsino� or other nome capitals, perhaps Antino�polis or even Alexandria, but they appear in this list from Karanis because they are landholders in the village or its horiodeiktia.

title: Fragment of a letter from Zenon to Protogenes
summary: The meaning of this fragment must remain obscure until other pieces of the same papyrus are identified.

title: Letter from Nikon (?) to Zenon
summary: The name of the writer is lost, but the hand is that of Nikon.  Apollonios had ordered him to take over some sheep from Artemidoros, and he asks Zenon to inform Apollonios that they have no sheep-pens and to give an order for the supply of building materials.

title: List of articles
summary: This list was drawn up at Hermopolis on the voyage down from Memphis to Alexandria.  The heading says, we have left behind the following articles which Charmos has handed over to Apollodotos.  Charmos is well-known as a commercial agent of Apollonios and Zenon.

title: Personal description
summary: A slip of papyrus containing a description of perhaps a runaway slave.

title: Letter from Nikon to Zenon
summary: Nikon had left some dishes in the hands of Artemidoros and now asks Zenon to get the price of them from Artemidoros and send it to him, or, if they have not been sold, to return them.
date_text: Date of reception: About 30th May, 257 BC

title: Letter to Zenon
summary: The writer acknowledges receipt of a consignment of shoots (?), brought to Philadelphia by donkey, and informs Zenon that he is bringing water from Tanis to irrigate the young olive trees.

title: Letter to Zenon
summary: The writer seems to be speaking about work on the Philadelphian estate and about certain dificulties which he has encountered.  He  mentions Artemidoros and Zoilos the Arsinoite.

title: Letter from Lykourgos to Zenon
summary: Lykourgos had already written to Zenon asking him to recover a sum of money from Agathokles after giving back to him some silver plate.  If Zenon has not carried out the former request, he is now to give the plate to Hermias, the bearer of the letter, who will himself recover the money. As Zenon notes in the docket that the plate is to be given to Hermias, we may infer that he had paid no attention to the former letter.
date_text: Date of reception: About 16th June, 257 BC

title: Letter from Toubias to Apollonios
summary: Toubias the Ammonite chief announces that, in response to Apollonios' letter, he has sent to the king, who was a great collector of strange animals, a gift consisting of horses, dogs, asses, and various speciments of cross-breeding with the wild ass, all of them domesticated. He also sends a letter to the king about the gift, to be delivered through Apollonios; and to reassure him about the contents of this letter he gives a copy of it below. It contains in fact nothing but a list of the animals.

title: Letter from Toubias to Apollonios
summary: In this letter, written on the same day as PCZ.59075 and received along with it on June 16th, Toubias informs Apollonios that he has sent him (not for the king, but for himself) a eunuch and four boy slaves of a superior class, whose descriptions are given below.

title: List of Payments in Kind
summary: A record of phaselus beans and other vegetables, possibly contributed for the military annona.

title: Letter from Krotos to Zenon
summary: Krotos  complains that Alexis has taken no steps with regard to the judgment given against the persons who sailed in charge of the oil, neither repaying the money nor handing over the slave girl whom they had offered as security.  Moreover he has let Theron, the sailor from the kubaia, escape from prison and disappear from sight.

title: Letter from Charmos to Zenon
summary: Charmos was an agent employed by Zenon on all sorts of business.  This letter is so badly preserved that it is difficult to say where and in what circumstances it was written.

title: Fragment
summary: The text seems to have contained a list of articles, with a note of reception at the top, Received in the Arsinoe of Dion, year 29, Daisios.

title: Letter from Iollas to Zenon
summary: A fragmentary letter from Iollas the weaver about Addaios, who appears to have been an agent of Apollonios at Memphis.  Iollas had fallen into disgrace and had been severely flogged.  He had intended to run away to Zenon for protection, but Zenodoros had prevented him and made him write a letter instead.
date_text: Date of reception: About 21st July, 257 BC

title: Letter from Moschos to Zenon
summary: A letter from Moschos to Zenon, about half of which is missing.  Andronikos, probably the oikonomos at Pelusium, is mentioned.
date_text: Date of reception: About 21st July, 257 BC

title: Letter from Philokrates to Zenon
summary: Philokrates, probably an employee of Apollonios, announces that he has sent the travellers a consignment of fish.
date_text: Date of reception: About 21st July, 257 BC

title: Letter from Peisikles to Zenon
summary: This is a statement of the price of the fish bought by Philokrates for the travellers and described by him in PCZ.59082.
date_text: Date of reception: About 21st July, 257 BC

title: Letter from Philon to Zenon
summary: The author is Philon the baker, who according to the docket writes about payment for work and informs Zenon that he has sent him an artaba and a half of flour.  Other matters were apparently mentioned in the letter, but we have at present only a fragment, the right half and the lower part being still missing.
date_text: Date of reception: About 21st July, 257 BC

title: Order from Philon to Sobisios
summary: Philon the baker asks Sosibios to give Midas thirty bundles of dry reeds for firewood.

title: Order from Philon to Sobisios
summary: Philon the baker asks Sosibios to give Midas thirty bundles of dry reeds for firewood.

title: Pittakion List
summary: A list of members of a pittakion headed by Isidorus, and associated amounts of money, probably payments.

title: Account of linen
summary: An account of linen garments and napkins issued from the store over a period of fourteen months, and it was presumably preceded by a statement of the quantities which the writer had in his charge (PCZ.59088).  The articles were given out for the most part during the tour of Apollonios and Zenon, and not only are the entries dated, but the scribe usually mentions the place where they were staying at the time. As Zenon followed the same practice in docketing his correspondence during these months, we can make a fairly good itinerary of the first tour, which ended at Alexandria in Xandikos of year 29.

title: Account of rugs
summary: The upper part of an account of rugs received and issued from the store during the tour of Apollonios.

title: Account of myrrh
summary: In lines 1-6 the writer states that he has received from Zenon a certain quantity of Mendesian myrrh in small lead vessels.  In lines 7-21 he gives an account of the quantities which he has dispensed to various persons in the first four months of year 29 after Apollonios' party had returned to Alexandria.

title: Account
summary: The upper part of an account of money received by Zenodoros through Peisikles.

title: Account
summary: Only the beginning of the account is preserved.  It refers to the time when Zenon was staying in Alexandria.
