title: List of Zenon's clothes
summary: This list of the contents of Zenon's portmanteau was given or sent to him by Peisikles, and perhaps the clothes described in it were his outfit for one of his long journeys with Apollonios.  They are of various kinds and colours, both for summer and for winter, for indoors and outdoors, some new, some worn and others partly worn. Evidently the chlamys and chiton were the most important garments, as he possessed six of the former, and no less than eleven of the latter, two of them with long sleeves.

title: Letter from Herakleitos to Zenon
summary: After describing his troubles in acquiring a new horse, Herakleitos proceeds, Apollophanes also has arrived in Syria and told him that Krotos is waiting in Joppa for an opportunity of exporting �� and mattresses.  He replied that he could not sail there at present, for he had been ordered by Dionysodoros to bring his baggage to Herakleia. He informed us too that those at home were all well. And Menekles of Tyre, he said, had brought some slaves and merchandise from Gaza to Tyre and landed them in Tyre for transshipment without notifying the Customs officers and without having a permit to export the slaves, and on learning this they had taken them from him. So Apollophanes coming to the aid of Menekles declared that the slaves and the merchandise were yours, and Menekles was obliged to back him up. The passage is far from clear, but the meaning seems to be that the declaration was a dodge to get the slaves out of the hands of the Customs officers. Herakleitos ends with a complaint about some delay in giving him an appointment, saying that Nikanor has treated him as if he were an enemy.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Kleitarchos, an agent of Damis, and Diodoros, employed on the estate of Apollonios, have received from the threshing-floor of Inaros, a native of Moithymis, one artab of Syrian wheat, 27 1/2 artabs of local wheat and 45 1/3 artabs of barley.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Letter from Zoilos to Zenon
summary: Zoilos the Arsinoite oikonomos asks Zenon to write and inform him whether Apollonios means to spend the festival of Arsinoe at Krokodilopolis, or in that district, in order that he may make preparations for the visit in good time.

title: Pittakion List
summary: On the recto are the remains of three accounts.  On the verso is a list of members of a pittakion directed by Isidorus.  The loss of the right side of the verso has obscured the purpose for which the list was compiled.

title: Letter from Hierokles to Zenon
summary: A letter concerning the education of the boy Pyrrhos; a proposal seems to have been made that he should be sent to another master.

title: Letter from Eumikos to Zenon
summary: A letter announcing the dispatch of a consignment of fruit of various kinds.

title: Fragment of a letter from Pylasis
summary: A fragment of a letter from Pylasis, received by Zenon three days later.

title: Letter from Melanippos to Zenon
summary: A badly preserved letter, stating that a certain Ptolemaios (?) was coming to see Apollonios, and Zenon is asked to assist him.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: These two fragments probably belong together, forming a duplicate receipt for 14 drachmae paid for work to be done on a transverse embankment.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Part of a duplicate receipt

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Part of a duplicate receipt

title: Letter from Apollonios to Panakestor
summary: Apollonios orders Panakestor to take as much pea-seed and poppy-seed as he requires for sowing and to keep the remainder safely guarded.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Panakestor
summary: Apollonios informs Panakestor that he is not able to send him any wood and instructs him to buy up as much as he requires,  not only in the Fayoum but in the neighbouring nomes as well.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Panakestor
summary: It appears that Panakestor had asked Apollonios to let him have a boat on the river.  Apollonios objects that the expense would be too great, as the boat would be lying idle for most of the year, while the sailors would be drawing wages all the time.  But he offers to send him a boat if he can make an arrangement with some of the boatmen in Kerke, who will have the use of it on condition that they pay the sailors' wages and lend it to Panakestor whenever he needs it.

title: List of Payments in Money
summary: The title of the list has been almost completely obliterated.  What remains is sufficient only to identify wheat as the grain which is measured in artabas throughout the preserved portions of the list.  The list appears to be concerned with collections of arrears of the land tax, at the constant commutation rate of one talent for each artaba.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Panakestor
summary: A fragment of a letter from Apollonios to Panakestor, concerning the planting of suckers on Apollonios' land at Philadelphia.

title: Letter from Zoilos to Panakestor
summary: Zoilos the oikonomos asks Panakestor to send Komoapis the engineer to repair a damaged dyke in the village of Tanis.

title: Letter from Amyntas to Zenon
summary: The first part of the letter is about Patron, the captain of a boat which was cruising between Alexandria and Apollonios' temporary place of residence.  Amyntas has heard that Patron is accustomed to excuse himself when he arrives late by saying that he has been retarded by Amyntas not giving him the letters in good time. The next sentence is mutilated and rather mysterious; it may perhaps mean that Amyntas did not care to write to Apollonios because he was not sure whether the latter knew of what was really going on. The letter ends with a list of the provisions which Amyntas has sent from Alexandria.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Receipt from Nikias for ten obols with which to pay ten workmen for making or repairing embankments on the land which he occupies.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Receipt for wood, probably grown in the Fayoum.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Stotoetis acknowledges that he has received from Kleitarchos and Andron three artabs of barley as seed for the land which he is farming and a loan of three more artabs.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: An incomplete receipt for barley seed.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Philistos, an employee of Etearchos the nomarch, acknowledges that he has received twenty drachmae, as a loan, from Panakestor.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: A duplicate receipt.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Pelois acknowledges receipt of 6 drachmae, 4 obols, as a loan, for cutting down the brushwood on 10 arurae and the same amount for burning the stumps on the same piece of land.

title: Loan contract
summary: From cartonnage. Very cursive hand, same as BGU IV 1050, BGU IV 1057, BGU IV 1059.

title: Report of Payments in Money
summary: The recto preserves the ends of four lines from the lower right corner of a tax account.  The verso has a report from Aurelius Ptollas to Aurelius Isidorus to the effect that he has collected monies from six persons who are named in lines 6-11 and a seventh whose contribution is credited in line 12 to property belonging to Ptollas himself.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: The same type of receipt, for payment for maintenance of trees, as PCZ.59117.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Theon, son of Protarchos, acknowledges receipt of five drachmae for the weeding of fifteen arurae of shore-land.

title: Letter from Artemidoros to Zenon
summary: Artemidoros had deposited two silver dishes with Dionysodoros as security for some money advanced to him for travelling expenses.  He now sends Zenon the receipts for the dishes, asking him to settle with Dionysodoros and after recovering the dishes to forward them by any suitable messenger who may be coming that way.

title: Letter from Ktesias to Zenon
summary: Ktesias writes that he is unable to sell the two jars of caviar and that Hierokles advises him to use them for private consumption and not let them be wasted.  The pomegranate seed also is too old to sell.

title: Fragment of a letter from Ktesias to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Ktesias to Zenon, about payment of wages.  Hierokles, probably a brother of Ktesias, had already spoken to Zenon on the subject.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Letter from Panakestor to Zenon
summary: In answer to Zenon's letter, Panakestor informs him that Iason has brought him a thousand drachmae in copper.  He asks Zenon to send him the salaries for three months and a supply of wine.  He would be glad if Zenon could find time to come and see to the work. He has already sent down 3600 artabs of barley and is now sending down the rest. He is short of papyrus and asks Zenon to send him some.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: An approval of the order which Zenon had given that the olive and laurel shoots should be planted in the park.  The reference is to the park at Philadelphia where Zenon had now gone, or was going, to reside as superintendent of Apollonios' property.

title: Letter from Panakestor to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Panakestor to Zenon, about an order to have some land surveyed and to send the measurements.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: A duplicate receipt.

title: Receipt for Wheat
summary: A receipt for wheat issued by an otherwise unknown dekaprotos of the 1st and 6th toparchy through his agent Dioscorus.  The recipient is the veteran Asclepiades, probably the father of Heros.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Pasis acknowledges receipt of eight drachmae for the weeding of the land which he is farming.

title: Letter from Zenon to Panakestor
summary: Zenon informs Panakestor that he has sent him the poreion and a hundred drachmae, which was all the money that he could procure.  He requests Panakestor to send to Krokodilopolis two loads of barley to make chidra and also to send him some cabbage from  time to time.

title: Letter to Zenon containing copy of letter from Apollonios
summary: A farmer on Apollonios' land had been arrested because he had failed to pay some tax.  Zenon is asked to write to Boubalos and Spendates to set him at liberty until the tax-collectors arrive, in order that the land may be weeded.  The writer adds a copy of a letter addressed to Thrason and Paramonos by Apollonios, ordering them not to trouble the farmers in Tapteia about the salt-tax.
date_text: Date of the letter of Apollonios: 16th April 256 or 254 B.C.

title: Fragment of a letter from Melas to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Melas to Zenon, announcing the despatch of certain comestibles.
date_text: Date of reception: March or April, 256 B.C.

title: Letter from Mys to Zenon
summary: Mys writes about a certain Symbotes, perhaps a cleruch, for whom a piece of land had been selected but not yet measured out.  Owing to this state of things, Mys could take no steps with regard to the crops, which ran the risk of being ruined.  Symbotes, however, paid no heed to his remonstrances but continued to wrangle with the royal scribe, insisting that the land should be measured by another standard, which would make a difference of ten percent in its area. Zenon is asked to give instructions about this matter as soon as possible.

title: Royal Oath
summary: Part of the inner text of a duplicate deed, in which five Egyptian brickmakers swear to Zenon that they will remain in Philadelphia until they have fulfilled their contract.  In addition to the religious sanction there is a penalty clause, the details of

title: Letter from Agathinos to Zenon
summary: Theodoros had asked Agathinos to give  him some money, saying that Zenon had written to that effect, but Krotos had arrived and forbidden him to give it.  He now awaits instructions from Zenon.

title: Letter from Mys to Zenon
summary: A friendly letter from Mys expressing anxiety about Zenon's health.

title: Letter from Artemidoros to Zenon
summary: Artemidoros tells Mys to hand over the sheep to Nouraios and to give him a receipt for any hay that they take for the draught animals and also to order the guards to keep the sheep under observation.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: A duplicate receipt for four drachmae, paid in advance to a certain Horos for the excavation of fifty aoilia of earth, probably on the estate of Apollonios.

title: Receipt for Wheat
summary: A receipt for wheat issued by the dekaprotoi of the 4th and 5th toparchy for grain delivered to the granary of Ptolemais Nea.  The recipients are Ptolemaeus and Thaisarion, son and daughter respectively of Pancrates, and therefore father and aunt of Isidorus.

title: Part of duplicate receipt
summary: Part of a duplicate receipt

title: Part of duplicate receipt
summary: Fragment of a receipt.  Theon apparently received money for the work every few days.

title: Letter from Alexandros to Zenon
summary: Alexandros informs Zenon that he has inquired about the charges which Kritias has been making against Charidemos and has found them to be false.

title: Letter (from Mys?) to Zenon
summary: The writer tells Zenon how he has disposed of certain large quantities of grain.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios approves the distribution of Milesian wool to the slave-girls in Memphis, and tells Zenon to give orders to let them have as much as they need.  The wool came from a flock of Milesian sheep which Apollonios owned in the Fayoum and the girls were employed in a wool factory beloning to him in Memphis.

title: Letter from Nikanor to Zenon
summary: A letter concerning trade of spices from Asia, brought on camels.

title: Letter from Charmos to Zenon
summary: Charmos announces that he has sent Zenon an acknowledgment of the receipt of ten talents of iron by Asklepiades and Rodon.

title: Petition to Zenon from Sphragis
summary: Sphragis had been robbed on her way to Sophthis, a village in the Memphite nome, of a few articles which she was carrying, two garments, some wool and a little copper money.  She asks Zenon to write to Leontiskos, the chief policeman in that district, to give her back the things stolen, which were now reported to have been found.

title: Letter from Agathon to Zenon
summary: Agathon had received from Mestris, the mother of Menippos' wife, certain garments belonging to Zenon, a chlamys and two chitons, one of them with sleeves.  He has finished his work on them and asks to whom he is to hand them over to take to Zenon.

title: Letter from Mys to Zenon
summary: Mys asks Zenon to strike out from the estimate the quantities of corn of which Mys reports reception, in order that their accounts may not be at variance with each other.  Zenon had order him to measure out to Nikanor a certain quantity of lentils, but the latter claimed more and Mys asks for instructions.

title: Receipt for tim� purou
summary: A receipt acknowledging a payment of 1400 drachmas by Ptolemaeus, son of Pancrates and father of Isidorus, in the autumn of 283 A.D.  It is recorded as a commutation for wheat.

title: Letter from Hierokles to Zenon
summary: Hierokles announces that Kallikon has sent a slave to Herakleopolis on some business and that Ptolemaios has asked the slave to pass by Philadelphia in order that Zenon may buy and send him two himatia, which are said to be cheaper up the river than in Alexandria.

title: Letter from Artemidoros to Zenon
summary: The letter seems to be a complaint against Agathinos about a transaction in wine.  Zenon, who exercised authority at Memphis as well as at Philadelphia, is asked to foce him to pay and to exact an account from him on oath.

title: Letter from Theodoros to Zenon
summary: Theodoros had delivered 90 artabs of wheat to be transported to Alexandria and placed to his account.  Of these he had received, on Zenonss order and at various times, 55 artabs from Artemidoros the steward of the household, so that Zenon still owed him 5 artabs. He now demands the balance in order to repay Artemidoros the scribe, from whom he had borrowed corn. But the steward refused to deliver any more wheat, even if Zenon should order him. So he urges Zenon to see that the wheat is delivered, as otherwise he will hold him responsible for the price of it.

title: Fragment of petition from a bee-keeper
summary: Part of a petition from a bee-keeper who seems to have got into trouble with the tax-collector.

title: Letter from Charmos to Zenon
summary: Charmos reports that he has received from Zopyrion the male pigs and given them to the swineherd to take to Philadelphia, but that Zopyrion is reluctant to give up the sow, pretending that she is too ill.  He asks Zenon to write to Zopyrion to hand over the sow and her litter of young pigs.
date_text: Date of reception: 15th or 19th October, 256 B.C.

title: Fragment of a letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Part of an order to see that the houses for the cadets or recruits are finished without delay.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios orders Zenon to send down to Alexandria a cargo of dry logs, as many and as thick as possible, to be used for the festival of Isis.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios writes that the king has bidden him sow a second crop on the land.  After harvesting the early corn Zenon is to water the land, erecting shadoufs if necessary, and to keep it flooded for not more than five days, and as soon as it dries he is to inform Apollonios when he will be able to harvest the first crop.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Zenon is ordered to take from Memphis, from Apollonios' own garden and from the palace grounds, pear shoots and young plants, as many as possible, and also to get some sweet-apple trees from Hermaphilos, all to be planted in the orchards at Philadelphia.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios orders Zenon to plant fir-trees all over the park and round the vineyard and the olive-groves, in the number of at least three hundred.  For the tree has a striking appearance and will be of service to the king.  That is to say, it would provide timber for ship-building besides being an ornament to the estate.

title: Receipts for tim� purou and oinou
summary: Three receipts.  The first records two payments on January 20th, 291 A.D.  Isidorus, son of Ptolemeaus, has paid 800 drachmae for arrears on the grain tax of 288/289, and Patieis, probably a son of Isidorus, has paid another 400 drachmae on his behalf.  The second receipt, dated August 21st, 294 A.D., credits Sarapion, son of Onnophris, with 400 drachmae paid to the account of 292/293. The third receipt acknowledges payment on November 18th, 294 A.D. of 316 drachmae as commutation for the annona of wine, plus transportation charges.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Zenon is ordered to see that the ploughing cattle are put out to grass,  as well as the mules and donkeys.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios reminds Zenon that it is time to plant the vines and the olives and the other shoots.  Zenon is therefore to send for a supply from Memphis and the other places and to give orders to begin planting.  Apollonios promises to send him from the Alexandrian district more vine-shoots and whatever other sorts of fruit-trees may be useful.

title: Letter from Nikon to Zenon
summary: Nikon asks Zenon to write to Nikanor to repay some oil which Nikon had given him for the use of the men.  He begs Zenon to send him help (in the form of money, no doubt) as he is nearly destitute and is conscious of being in disgrace with Apollonios.  Menemachos has urged him to sail down to Alexandria and see Apollonios, promising to speak on his behalf and advising him to talk about the work at Philadelphia. Zenon is asked to write if he approves of this idea and also to send him some corn in order that he may not be obliged to buy at a high price.

title: Letter from Kleon (?) to Zenon
summary: Kleon had been meaning to send for some pigs to sell and had spoken to Damis suggesting that (as he himself was starting on a journey and had no messenger free) the latter should write to Zenon and ask whether he was to buy for him too.  Damis replied that he would wait till Kleon came back and buy for Zenon and himself in common. Kleon had now returned and had put at his disposal 59 pigs at the current price of 4 drachmae and half an obol each. He has written to keep Zenon informed.

title: Fragment of a letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios informs Zenon that he has sent him a large consignment of shoots and young trees of vine and pomegranate, (as he had promised to do in PCZ.59159).

title: Fragment of a letter to Zenon
summary: An almost illegible fragment of a letter, probably from Panakestor to Zenon.

title: Letter from Panakestor to Zenon
summary: Panakestor thanks Zenon for sending the donkeys.  He has received also ten mattocks and he will try to hire donkey-drivers from his own district.

title: Fragment of a letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Fragment of a letter from Apollonios to Zenon, apparently about the feeding of the calves on Apollonios' estate.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios asks Zenon to send him a list of all the yokes of cattle, female and male, so that he may buy as many more as are needed and send them to Philadelphia.

title: Report about the harvest
summary: Pasis the foreman of Zenon's farmers reports about the corn that has been carried to the threshing-floor from the 1st to the 10th of Mecheir.

title: Receipts for Barley
summary: Two receipts issued to Sarapion, son of Onnophris, who is represented by Isidorus, son of Ptolemaeus.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios orders Zenon to build a temple of Sarapis as a companion to that of Isis, giving details about its position with regard to other temples.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: A distinguished visitor called Antikritos was coming to look round, and Apollonios asks Zenon to show him the chief points of interest and to impress on him that they have only lately begun to found the settlement.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios announces that he has sent from the Heliopolite nome a hundred jars of wine which Zenon is to sell at a good price, using the proceeds to pay for the weaving of carpets.  He wishes to know how the wine sells and whether he ought to send more, as he still has plenty of it in the aforesaid nome.

title: Fragment of a letter from Diotimos to Zenon
summary: Diotimos asks Zenon to accompany a certain person to Herakleopolis, probably a distinguished visitor and possibly an ambassador from abroad.

title: Memorandum to Zenon from Komoapis
summary: Komoapis reports that he has given out to a number of separate contractors the work to be done on a road or canal, no doubt in the neighbourhood of Philadelphia.  There was a separate contract for each schoinion and the accepted tenders vary from 2 1/2 to 7 2/3 drachmae, showing that some sections were more easily worked than others.

title: Duplicate contract about a money loan
summary: A contract stating that Zenon had lent six farmers 10 drachmae each and three others 8 drachmae each, to buy donkeys for the purpose of carrying in their crops.  The farmers were Egyptians who rented land on the estate of Apollonios at Philadelphia and the money was to be repaid before the end of Pachon along with the rent, or else a donkey, approved by Zenon, was to be handed over instead of it. In case of default Zenon could exact restitution from them and their guarantors. Each farmer is guaranteed by a friend, one of which is a Greek. Then follow the names of the six witnesses, four Greeks and two Egyptians, one of the latter being the town clerk of Philadelphia.

title: Fragment of a letter from Sosos to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter to Zenon, from Sosos, a business agent of Zenon or Apollonios.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Daily account of receipts and expenses
summary: This long papyrus, described on the verso as ephemerides, gives an account of receipts and expenses, entered and balanced day by day, for a period of nearly a month.  It is written on the recto only, across the fibres, and the entries have been checked.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: In the fragmentary letter Apollonios orders Zenon to send him samples of various crops which he had been growing at Philadelphia.

title: Receipt for Wheat
summary: Parts of several receipts for wheat, three of which include a payment of the obols and probably of the diagraphai, in addition to the collection of wheat.

title: Fragment of a letter from Apollonios (?)
summary: A fragment of a letter from Apollonios (?).

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios writes to Zenon on the subject of certain vineyards which formed part of his d�rea in the Memphite nome, but which certain cleruchs there claimed as their property.  The case had been sent for trial before a judge called Krataimenes.  Apparently the cleruchs failed to put in an appearance, and in consequence of this Apollonios had written two letters, of which he sends copies to Zenon for guidance. One is addressed to Krataimenes, asking him to give an order that the crop whould be kept under watch, and the other is to Paramonos, perhaps an overseer of the memphite d�rea, instructing him to see that the crop is kept safe after being gathered in. It can be inferred that the judge had not yet given a definite decision and that the trial had been postponed owing to the absence of the cleruchs.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Zenon had spent two hundred drachmae in copper on binding the hay and Apollonios writes to say that he approves.

title: Letter from Ktesias to Zenon
summary: Ktesias asks Zenon to send him some money, as at present he is borrowing from Charmides.

title: Duplicate contract
summary: Three fragments of a duplicate contract attested by six witnesses.  A number of peasants acknowledge that they have received from Zenon an advance of money, at the rate of two drachmae for each aroura, for the cutting of the brush-wood on the land which t

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios orders Zenon to take at least 3000 olive shoots from his park and from the gardens at Memphis.  Before the fruit is gathered he is to mark each tree from which he intends to take shoots, and he is to choose above all the wild olive and the laurel, for the Egyptian olive is only suitable for parks and not for olive-groves.

title: Fragment of a letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Apollonios giving an order about the bakers and fifty artabs of wheat, in connection with the feast of the Arsinoeia which took place towards the end of Mesore.

title: Letter from Onnophris to Zenon
summary: Onnophris reports that on receiving Zenon's letter he has gone fowling with Eutychides but up to the present has not been able to send him more than thirty kerk�reis and twenty larger birds.

title: Fragment of a letter from Nikon to Zenon
summary: Nikon writes that he or his men have received no allowance of table-oil or lamp-oil for some time and goes on to speak of the damage that is being done in his district.

title: Receipt for Delivery of Emmer
summary: This receipt consists of two parts.  In lines 1 - 3 Isidorus, son of Ptolemaeus, in his capacity of kephalaiotes is credited with delivery to a bakery, presumably military, of an unspecified quantity of emmer entrusted to him by the sitologoi.  The delivery includes both current dues and arrears. Lines 4-5 have a similar acknowledgment of delivery by Heroninus, also in his capacity of kephalaiotes.

title: Memorandum from Otearmotes
summary: Otearmotes asks that instructions be given to Zenon to measure the land in Tapeptia with the schoinion used in the Memphite nome and also that an order be given about the sycamore boat  in accordance with a previous agreement.

title: Fragment of petition to Zenon from Seleukos
summary: Probably a complaint about being taxed too heavily by the authorities in the Memphite nome and a request to Zenon to put things right.

title: Fragment of a letter (from Artemidoros to Zenon?)
summary: A fragment of a letter probably to Zenon from Artemidoros the physician in Alexandria with reference to his property at Philadelphia.

title: Letter from Artemidoros to Zenon
summary: Artemidoros asks Zenon to send him, by any of the boats that may be coming down the river, a supply of firewood and charcoal and also some sacrificial pigs for himself and the paidiskai, in preparation for the great festival of Isis which was celebrated in the month of Athyr.

title: Letter from Platon to Zenon
summary: Platon writes that the youth called Demetrios who is delivering the letter has a father living in the Fayoum and would like to find some work there himself and he asks Zenon to give him employment of some sort and to look after him.  He adds that he has sent Zenon two artabs of chick-peas and will try and buy some more at Naukratis and bring them up the river himself.

title: Letter (from Diotimos?) to Zenon
summary: The writer gives Zenon some instructions about a house which was being built for him.  As the courtyard was too small and a stable had not been included, the architect at his request was going to increase the length of the site by twenty cubits.  He asks Zenon to give orders to this effect and to begin work at once. They will not be delayed by lack of wood. The architect has been ordered to leave a space for a garden round the house and to change the position of the gateway and bakery.

title: Letter from Demetrios to Zenon
summary: Diotimos had sent word to Demetrios to give certain large jars to a messenger from Zenon, to enable him to receive the price for the month of Phaophi.  Zenon had sent the messenger, and 410 jars had been handed over to him.  Demetrios now asks Zenon to send him the money or, if not all of it, at least half. As the price of the jars was twenty drachmae for twelve, the sum due was 683 drachmae, 2 obols.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon and Panakestor
summary: Apollonios informs his two agents that he has sent Maron to take charge of the Milesian sheep.  They are to hand over the sheep and all the material and introduce him to the local shepherds who will be under his orders.  Four of the boys belonging to the belonging to the estate are to be given to him to learn his methods.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter, apparently about planting shoots, but not from Apollonios.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Receipt for Various Taxes
summary: Receipts for various taxes, some issued by Sitologoi, some by Apaitetai, covering a ten year period.  Most of the receipts in the roll were issued to Palemon, son of Ptolemaeus and brother of Isidorus.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: One of the brewers in the Arsinoite nome has offered to take over the beer-house in Philadelphia with the obligation of brewing, to the profit of the Crown, a supply of barley provided by the Crown and amounting to twelve artabs a day.  Zenon is therefore ordered to draw up a contract, get a written oath from him and hand over the brewery. But he is also to instal a reliable collector to exercise control. And the present brewer must settle up for the period during which he has run the business.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Zenon is ordered to erect a building of some sort at Philadelphia and the architect is to see that it is properly equipped.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: A note from Apollonios the dioiketes to say that he will shortly send Apollonios the contractor to Zenon.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Zenon has done right to arrest the treasurer attached to the beer-house.  Apollonios has sent Amenneus the brewer to Philadelphia in order that the treasurer may be confronted with him and convict him of the charges which he has brought against him.  The case is to be tried before Peton the chrematistes, who is coming specially for this and for another affair (see PCZ.59203 and 04). If Amenneus appears to have really spoken as reported, Apollonios threatens that he will have him hanged.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Apollonios writes that he has sent Zenon a copy of the letter which he has just written to the native peasants in Hephaistias ordering them to appear at Philadelphia by day-break and not let Peton the chrematistes be detained.  The copy is subjoined.  In it he informs the peasants that he is too busy to hear the case himself but has sent Peton instead. They are therefore to meet him at Philadelphia early next morning in order that he may hear their pleadings and those of Sopatros the agent of Damis, so that, if the latter is proved to be guilty, he may receive the punishment he deserves.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: As soon as Peton the chrematistes arrives in Philadelphia, Zenon is to show him a lodging and give him supplies for one day.  For after hearing the two cases he will return immediately to Apollonios.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: The writer had previously addressed a letter to Kriton the stolarch asking him to obtain a lodging for him in Philadelphia and to do something for his messenger Herakleides.  But as he may have arrived before Kriton, he now sends Zenon a copy of the letter and asks him to be good enough to provide the lodging at once.

title: Account of tax-payments
summary: An account of the collection of certain taxes by Apollonios' agents from tax-payers living on his estates.
date_text: Probably drawn up in the spring of 254 B.C.

title: Letter from Onnophris to Zenon
summary: Onnophris was one of the farmers or foremen whom Zenon employed to cultivate land and more especially such land as he had taken over from the neighbouring cleruchs.  He had asked Zenon for a cart and perhaps a camel to carry the crop on the holding of Ptolemaios, and he now presses his request. In the second part of the letter he speaks about work to be done on the land of Theopompos.

title: Receipt for Delegatio
summary: A receipt issued in the Autumn of 303 A.D. to Isidorus, presumably the son of Ptolemaeus, who was collector of the delegatio for Karanis in 300/301 A.D.  He is credited with turning in three talents on October 23rd and 500 drachmae on the following day.

title: Fragment of a letter from Apollonios
summary: A fragment of a letter from Apollonios.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: Apparently an order to Zenon to prevent the excape of a certain logeut�s.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Fragment of a letter to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter to Zenon.

title: Letter from Ptolemaios to Zenon
summary: Ptolemaios asks Zenon to recover and hand over to the bearer a horse which a slave of his had run away with and sold.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Letter from Dioskourides to Zenon
summary: Dioskourides writes that he has received twelve yoke of cattle, as well as the pack donkeys, but that four of the twelve yoke are useless.  He then enumerates the eight defective animals and complains that he will be obliged to feed them without being able to use them for the sowing.

title: Fragment of a letter from Dikaios to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Dikaios to Zenon.

title: Letter from Platon to Zenon
summary: Platon writes that as he has been obliged to stay some time longer in Alexandria, he would like to sell his corn in Memphis and he asks Zenon to lend him a boat to carry it down and also to see that his affairs are conducted profitably.  He also asks him to order one of his people to procure a pig for the festival.

title: Loan contract
summary: Verso.Same hand as BGU IV 1053, BGU IV 1058.

title: Receipt for Chaff
summary: A receipt issued to two collectors of chaff (Hatres and ?Isidorus) at Karanis by a receiver at Leukogion, a village in the Hercleopolite nome which served the Fayum as a southern port on the Nile.

title: Memoranda
summary: A series of imperfect memoranda, containing a list of the members of a certain Egyptian family and a list of the priests who have paid their portions.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: An order to give out to contractors the work on the canal to the west of the ten thousand arourai, which formed the estate of Apollonios at Philadelphia.

title: Fragment of a letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Apollonios to Zenon.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: A fragmentary letter from Apollonios on the planting of fruit-trees on his estate at Philadelphia.

title: Letter from Thrasymedes to Zenon
summary: Thrasymedes reports that Etearchos having arrived on the 7th of Phaophi, a receipt had been written to Python for the 100 artabs of sesame which Zenon had measured out in the preceding year, as well as for the 105 artabs of knekos measured out on the 10th

title: Fragment of petition to Zenon from Teos
summary: A fragment of petition to Zenon from Teos, possibly about a robbery.

title: Letter from Artemidoros to Zenon
summary: A letter from Artemidoros the physician.  He had heard that the sons of Leptines in Pharbaithos, Nikandros and Myrikon, had a black horse with large swellings on the legs which rendered him useless except to breed from, and he asks Zenon to use his influence with the young men in order to procure him the horse, either buying him outright for a small sum or borrowing him for the breeding season, as his own stallion is rather old and wanting in vigour. He also reminds Zenon about the sesame on the kl�ros, asking him to see it harvested and inform him of the amount of the crop.

title: Letter from Diotimos to Zenon
summary: Diotimos announces that he has sent back Eutychides, who had apparently been summoned to explain certain accounts, and that he is going to Ptolemais on the 10th to transact business there.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: Date from a long-lined letter.

title: Receipt for Barley and Transportation Charges
summary: A series of seven receipts for barley delivered at the harbours of Kerke and Leukogion by the sitologoi of Karanis as part of the canon, or grain levy, imposed on the village for the year 308/309.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter apparently about the leasing of pigs to native swineherds.

title: Letter from Timokles to Sostratos
summary: The writer, Timokles, says that he has sent two workmen to stake the vines and that two others will join them in three days' time.  He asks Sostratos to supply them with bast and stakes.

title: Letter from Zenon to Sostratos
summary: Zenon asks his friend and partner Sostratos to send him one of the masons to point out the brick-makers and the other masons, but to warn this man not to disclose himself lest they should all decamp.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Fragment of a corn account
summary: On the verso is a column giving the quantities of various grains received piecemeal, but much of it is almost illegible.

title: Letter from Artemidoros to Zenon
summary: Artemidoros speaks of laying the foundation of some building and begs Zenon to send him the architect.  He also asks him to send the fir trees to plant in the garden.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Fragment of letter from Phileas to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Phileas (?) to Zenon, perhaps concerning the sale of property.

title: Petition to Diotimos from Neoptolemos
summary: A petition presented by Neoptolemos on behalf of his father Stratippos, a vine-grower.  He complains that Stratippos had been unfairly treated by Theokles the late oikonomos and Petosiris the royal scribe of the Aphroditopolite nome.  For in assessing the taxes to be paid on vineyards they had been taking as a basis the average yield of the last three years, but in his father's case they had taken the last two years only, on the ground that his vineyard was lately planted. He therefore asks Diotimos to give him an order addressed to Hermolaos (the present oikonomos) and Petosiris, bidding them assess his father's vineyard like the others on the average yield of three years, beginning either from year 29 or year 30, as it had now been producing wine for four years. He also asks him to accept formally as payment of the tax the money, or part of the money, paid into the bank by the wine-sellers for the wine which they bought from the vineyard.

title: Letter from Dionysodoros to Zenon
summary: Knowing that Zenon was buying honey for Nikon, Dionysodoros asks him to buy a metretes for himself and send it along with Nikon's informing him at the same time to whom he should pay the price.

title: Receipts for Wheat
summary: Three receipts issued by different shipmasters to Ision, Pamiton, and their associate receivers; each receipt acknowledges a delivery of wheat to the harbour of Kerke in the Memphite nome by Kopres and his fellow sitologoi of Karanis for the year 308/309.

title: Letter from Phileas to Zenon
summary: An almost indecipherable letter from Phileas to Zenon, apparently about building operations.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Letter from Kleanax to Zenon
summary: Kleanax writes to Zenon about certain mules which he had sent his slaves to buy and hand over to Zenon in order that they might be sent down along with those of Apollonios to the d�rea of the latter in Memphis.  The object of this manoeuvre, as he frankly explains, was that they might pass as Apollonios' property and escape being taxed for export and transit. He wishes to know if they have come to hand and been dispatched and Zenon has settled the matter; and if they have not yet come to hand, he begs him to give orders to his people to do as he, Kleanax, has requested.

title: Letter from Zenon to Krotos
summary: Zenon orders Krotos to obtain twenty five minae of wool from Pasis the Jew and to contract with Artemidoros for the making of a mattress, which was to be at least long enough for a seat for two and to have a double front.  The mattress was required for Peisikles, and Krotos was to try to have it finished in fifteen days.

title: Letter from Apollonios to Zenon
summary: Zenon is ordered to prepare the fittings for the galleys called taurokerkouroi, while Theon will put them on board a boat and bring them down the river.  He must expedite the work, for an urgent message has come to Apollonios to set about the dispatch of the galleys to Alexandria in preparation for the up-voyage of the king's daughter.

title: Letter from Horos to Zenon
summary: Horos was one of the farmers whom Zenon employed to look after the land which he exploited, particularly that which he leased from the cleruchs.  This Egyptian overseer, who writes or dictates a very halting Greek, informs Zenon that there be 120 arourai planted with poppy by the 12th of Choiak, a sight to please his eyes. On the back of the letter is a note in the same hand about a kl�ros which he apparently wished Zenon to lease: we have got a holding to the north; it gives us twenty arourai on which to plant kiki; let Zenon take two thirds and the owner keep one third.

title: Letter from Hermon to Zenon
summary: Hermon had written that he was sending Zenon a certain number of logs and olive shoots and he now adds that, after the first letter was written, both consignments had been increased.  Among the logs are seven shoots which it is doubtful if Zenon will accept. If he likes them, he is to write and Hermon will send another consignment of them. The letter ends with a request for more money.

title: Letter from Kollouthes to Zenon
summary: Kollouthes writes that after leaving Zenon he found that the peasants belonging to the land which had been portioned out among the soldiers had fled to the temple of Isis in the Memphite nome.  So at the time when he received Zenon's letter he was setting out for Krokodilopolis to ask Maimachos the monarch to rout them out; and as soon as this is done he will come to Zenon.

title: Fragment of a letter from Eutychides
summary: A fragment of a letter from Eutychides.

title: Letter from Philiskos to Zenon
summary: Philiskos, the present oikonomos of the Arsinoite province, writes that he had been about to set out for Philadelphia when a letter arrived ordering him to go to Ptolemais to meet Ariston, a member of the Court, who had sailed up to see the  nome; but he expects to be with Zenon on the 17th. He asks him therefore to send Hedylos to Krokodilopolis in order that they may draw up a form of contract and then put the work up to tender in Philadelphia. He also tells Zenon to inform him whether he has received the mattocks from the Herakleopolite nome.

title: Receipt for Transportation Charges
summary: A receipt issued in the spring of 311 A.D. to Palemon, son of Ptolemaeus and brother of Isidorus, by two groups of collectors of transportation dues associated with the military annona.

title: Fragment of a contract
summary: A fragment of a contract containing reference to a priest and priestess.

title: Fragment of a letter to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter to Zenon.

title: Letter from Promethion to Zenon
summary: Part of a letter to Zenon from Promethian, a banker at Mendes in the Delta.

title: Letter from Artemidoros to Zenon
summary: Artemidoros the physician writes that he and Apollonios are both well and that they are now returning to Sidon after having escorted the queen as for as the frontier.  He expects to be in Philadelphia very soon and asks Zenon to do various things for him; to buy some honey and barley, to look after his house and see that it is roofed over, to keep an eye on his cattle, pigs, geese and other farm stock and to attend to the harvesting of his crops. [The first few lines of the letter refer to the wedding of the princess Berenike. She had been accompanied by her father as far as Pelusium, where the king took leave of her and entrusted her to the care of Apollonios. At the frontier line between Syria and the Syrian province of Egypt she was met by Antiochos or his ambassadors and the Egyptian mission returned to Sidon and thence to Egypt. Apollonios, to whom the king had apparently paid the high compliment of choosing him to act as his representative on this historic occasion, took with him as usual his private physician Artemidoros.]

title: Fragment of a farm account
summary: A fragment of a farm account, refering to land in the village of Pharbaithos.

title: Letter from Poseidonios (?) to Zenon
summary: The writer states that Pyron has been given Zenon's last private account and has taken note of certain details of which he had been ignorant.  He has been detained several days because the writer was busy.

title: Letter from Phanios to Zenon
summary: Phanias informs Zenon that he has decided to review all the recruits who have received allotments in the Arsinoite nome and to administer the oath to them in Philadelphia.  He asks therefore that a lodging be prepared for him, as he is in poor health and would llike to be with Zenon as long as possible.

title: Fragment of a letter to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter to Zenon.

title: Letter from Philiskos to Zenon
summary: A letter from Philiskos the chief oikonomos of the Arsinoite province.  Before Zenon's letter reached him he had heard from the people in Tanis that there was some trouble about the water.  He has therefore sent work to Maimachos the nomarch to repair the mouth of the canal and he writes to Zenon to keep him informed.

title: Acknowledgment of a loan
summary: Asklepiades, a cleruch whose land was leased by Zenon, acknowledges receipt of four drachmae, the value of which in corn is to be deducted from the rent in kind due to him in the following season.  The land was in the neighbourhood of Moithymis and the re

title: Receipts for Various Taxes
summary: Four receipts (two columns, with two receipts in each) issued to Kyrillous, daughter of Kopres.  She was also the sister of Ta�sis, who was the wife of Heras, brother of Isidorus.

title: Letter of receipt from Demophon to Sokles
summary: Demophon, one of the numerous cleruchs whose land was leased to Zenon and cultivated by his farmers, acknowledges receipt of 40 artabs of wheat, being the rent for year 34.  The receipt is addressed to Sokles, an agent.

title: Letter from Maimachos to Zenon
summary: A letter from Maimachos in which he speaks of Kallippos, a shepherd, in connection with pasture land.

title: Letter from Kleitarchos
summary: Kleitarchos, an employee of Zenon, orders someone to measure out to the son of Alkimos from Hephaistias 30 artabs of black poppy seed.

title: Memorandum to Zenon from the fishmongers
summary: The fish merchants present a bill for 180 drachmae, being the price of 450 fish.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter to Zenon from a friend who, like himself, was a dog-fancier.

title: Letter from Philon to Zenon
summary: Philon says that Maiandria has written to him about a cloak which Zenon has ordered her to weave.  At present she is ill, but as soon as she is better Zenon shall have the garment.  The writer adds that Apollonios has taken over the direction of public affairs and that Dionysodoros is acting as chief accountant.

title: Letter from Sisouchos to Zenon
summary: Zenon had told Sisouchos to see the potter about the jars.  If he wishes them to be done at once, he is to write to the potter in order that Sisouchos and the latter may come to him before the time slips away.  He has sent Zenon ten chamber-pots and asks him to write if ever he wants anything else.

title: Receipt for a loan
summary: Nektosiris, a gardener from the Aphroditopolite nome, acknowledges that he has received from Zenon a loan of money as stipulated in the contract kept by Nestos, on condition that he will repay half by a certain date and the remainder by another date.

title: Duplicate receipt
summary: Krotos acknowledges receipt of two fowls and twenty-five eggs.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Receipt for the Price of Clothing
summary: A receipt addressed to Antonius Sarapammon, strategos of the Arsinoite nome, by Isidorus, son of Palenius, and Doulus, son of Timotheus, komarchs of Karanis, and Isidorus, tesserarius of the same village.  The officials of Karanis acknowledge that they haacknowledge that they have received payment from the bankers who handle the government funds in the Arsinoite nome, in accordance with a warrant issued by the strategos, for 22 tunics and 8 cloaks which they delivered to the receivers, Cyrillus and Demeas. These articles constituted the village quota of a requisition of military clothing for the year 310/311, but payment for the contribution was delayed until the beginning of 314.

title: Agricultural account
summary: Part of an account of receipts and expenses presented by Pyrrhos, one of Zenon's farmers.

title: Account with Herakleides the vine-dresser
summary: Herakleides had contracted to pay 375 drachmae for the right of exploiting a vineyard of 37 1/2 arourai for one year.  Moreover he had bought vegetables, fruit and roses on a large scale and had entered into various other transactions, so that the whole amount of what he owed came to 851 drachmae, 5 obols. From this had to be deducted his wages for work on the vineyard in the next year and various other sums due to him, reducing his debt to 305 drachmae, 3 obols.

title: Letter from Spondates to Zenon
summary: Zenon had written to Spondates to send some sycamore wood to Kersat, and the latter promises to do so.  Palous was said to have reported that there was no acanthus wood; but in fact the muleteers had carried some from Mea on the 15th and he himself had borrowed a cart and taken some more on the 16th. What was really needed was more sycamore wood, and Spondates asks Zenon to send Theopompos to buy from the ibis-feeders from Mea, so that the boat-builders may have no excuse for making trouble.

title: Fragment of a letter from Neesis to Zenon
summary: Neesis, who was a potter, writes about certain jars which he has made, about wages for the workmen and about the preparation of ovens for the pitching of the pottery.

title: Letter from Timotheos to Zenon
summary: Timotheos had delivered Zenon's letter about the 1000 (?) drachmae which he had requested Python the banker to enter as paid by Timotheos.  Python, however, had made some objection which is not clear, and Timotheos asks Zenon to write to Python and press

title: Letter from Iphimedon to Zenon
summary: A letter from Iphimedon asking Zenon to send somebody to take over from a slave sent by Iphimedon two large and three small ovens for the calves.  Zenon is also to write and inform him what arrangement he has made about the kl�ros.

title: Account of pigs
summary: An account of pigs.

title: Letter from [�.]nis to Sesos
summary: The writer announces that certain persons have taken him to prison after searching and sealing up the house and after carrying off a quantity of meat.  Sesos is therefore requested to intercede with Zenon.  Similary a certain Achommneuis employed by Zenon has been put in prison by Horos the village scribe for some default in connection with the salt tax.

title: Letter from Timotheos (?) to Zenon
summary: The writer reports that  he has received the squared stones brought by Psemtheus and that he has handed them over to Dionysios, apparently to be transported to Alexandria or to some place on the river.

title: Fragment of a letter from Zenon to Artemidoros (?)
summary: A fragment of a letter from Zenon, possibly an order to Artemidoros, the banker of Philadelphia, to pay certain sums to certain people for irrigation work.

title: Receipts for Barley
summary: The text is arranged in two columns.  The first is a list of names under the rubric kephalaiotae of the village of Karanis, probably foremen in charge of the donkey caravan which moved the barley receipted in the second column from the granary at Karanis to a river port, where it was put on board ship for delivery to Alexandria. The second column consists of two receipts issued to Germanus and Ariston, komarchs of Karanis, of three shipments of barley, marked for delivery to the horsebreeder Hephaestion.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Letter from Pasis to Zenon
summary: There had been some previous correspondence about procuring arakos and Pasis now reports how the matter stands.

title: Letter to Zenon
summary: A badly preserved letter, apparently in answer to Zenon's request to take the depth of water at a certain place.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: It seems to be an invitation to Zenon to bring his friends Sostratos and Demeas to a sacrifice in honour of the king.

title: Letter from Hierokles to Zenon
summary: The writer asks Zenon to intercede on behalf of the bearer, probably a cleruch who was being threatened with confiscation and the exaction of certain arrears.

title: Letter from Hierokles to Zenon
summary: Hierokles, one of Zenon's Alexandrian correspondents, writes him a letter of recommendation on behalf of a certain Hermogenes who was coming as a cleruch to the Fayoum or to some neighbouring district.  If he is not stationed at Philadelphia, Zenon is requested to write to his commanding officer, wherever he may be. The letter was carried by Hermogenes himself and was not delivered till four months after the date on which it was written.

title: Letter from Hierokles to Zenon
summary: A request to Zenon to give the slave Onesimos fifty drachmae for a certain piece of business.

title: Fragment of a letter from Eudemos (?) to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Eudemos (?) to Zenon.

title: Letter from Zenon to Isingos
summary: Zenon informs Isingos that the Arabian fleeces which he has received from the latter are so rough and dirty that he has sent Herakleides back with them.  The letter was either returned by Isingos or never delivered.

title: Receipt for Money in Payment of a Requisition
summary: A receipt, written by Nilammon, a councilor, for Ariston and Germanus, komarchs of Karanis, addressed to Heracles, for payment for the three deliveries of barley noted in Isid.57.

title: Letter from Antipatros to Zenon
summary: Antipatros announces that he has sent Thrason the barber, whom the bearers of the letter from Memphis accuse of having maltreated them, in order that Zenon may examine him.  He adds that evidence is offered by several people that Thrason had been first wronged by the others.

title: Fragment of a letter to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter to Zenon.

title: Petition to Zenon from Harmais and Teos
summary: The petitioners were two Egyptian brick-makers.  They had been ordered by Zenon to work for four months, but had been kept working by Horos, Zenon's foreman, for a whole year extra and had been shamefully sweated.  They ask Zenon to inquire into their case and bring Horos to account.

title: Long corn account
summary: The subject of the account is the expenditure of various different grains on the property of Apollonios.  The lists and entries mention almost every kind of field-crop grown in Egypt at that time.  The corn was given out for sowing, for wages, for the feeding of pigs and dogs, and various other purposes.

title: Account of barley
summary: A note of the quantities of barley issued to various persons in years 33, 34 and 35.  The quantities were large, the total being nearly 36,600 artabs.

title: Fragment of memorandum
summary: On the recto is part of a letter or memorandum, from an Egyptian employee, perhaps a certain Petosiris.  On the verso is a list of Egyptians from whom or to whom money was owing.

title: Enumeration of women working wool
summary: A short list giving the  numbers of the women engaged in the working of wool, in accordance with the apograph� of year 35, in three villages in the Fayoum.

title: Account of payments
summary: An account of payments, partly for repairing of canals and stone cutting, and partly for guards.  Of these, three classes are specified: ten village guards of Philadelphia together with ten Arabs; five guards posted in the prison; six watchmen to look six watchmen to look after the canal embankments during the inundation. The regular guards have a salary of six drachmae a month, while the watchmen on the canal banks are only paid two and a half drachmae.

title: Memorandum about money
summary: A note about Zenon's balance at the bank.  Lines 1 - 11 give the sums which had been deposited to his credit.  Lines 13 - 28 state the amounts which he was paying to the Treasury through the bank, several of which are clearly payments on behalf of other people. The balance is struck in lines 29 - 30.

title: Letter from Rhodon to Paramonos
summary: Rhodon writes about various purchases which Paramonos had commissioned him to make, three choes of honey, a mattress for which he had given earnest-money without as yet obtaining the article, and a pig for the festival of Arsinoe.

title: Receipts for Various Taxes
summary: Four receipts (two columns, with two receipts in each); two issued to Heras, son of Ptolemaeus and brother of Isidorus, and his wife Ta�sis, daughter of Kopres; one issued to Ta�sis alone, and one in the name of Kyrillous, sister of Ta�sis.  The first three receipts acknowledge payment of charges assessed on land at Kerkesoucha and Ptolemais, villages which had close economic ties with Karanis.

title: Letter from Euempolos to Zenon
summary: Euempolos writes about sending Zenon supplies of garlic and about the cultivation of garlic in the neighbourhood of Philadelphia.

title: Letter from Euempolos to Zenon
summary: Euempolos reports that with Herakleides' apporoval he had been inspecting the vineyards and making a census of the persons who had planted vegetables in them, in order thay they should pay the h�miseuma, as usual, on a joint valuation of the crops.  The only man who objected was Apollonides, who declared that he would not allow him to enter his vineyard. But Apollonides, who declared that he would not be talking in this foolish way if he had been made to pay the proper amount for the sub-letting of the hundred-aroura vineyard. For whereas Euempolos had obtained 20 and 41 drachmae for quite small areas, putting them up to auction in the regular way, Apollonides had made a private arrangement with certain persons and then announced that he had let the whole for 50 drachmae; and he even made them a present of the grapes that were left. Euempolos proceeds to say that nobody is putting the old wine-vats in order or building new ones, although the vintage is drawing near, nor are they making ready to catch the mice. So unless Zenon comes at once and gives his orders, he will find himself much behindhand.

title: Memorandum to Zenon from Kassandros
summary: Kassandros reminds Zenon about a certain Egyptian about whom he had already spoken to him in Alexandria.  This man was one of the peasants from Psychis in the Memphite nome who had been taken to Philadelphia to work at the harvest, and Zenon had promised to let him go

title: Fragment of a contract (?)
summary: A fragment of a contract, apparently about some work at Philadelphia.  On the verso is a list of wine-jars and their prices.

title: Part of a letter to Zenon
summary: Part of a letter to Zenon.

title: Letter from Protarchos to Zenon
summary: Protarchos writes that, as Zenon had promised to send them a quantity of natron, they had ordered none themselves and the supply in the nome being finished they were obliged to travel about and obtain what they could from other nomes. He asks Zenon therefore to send for the natron at once, in order that the linen-cleaners may be able to continue their work.

title: Letter from Paramonos to Zenon
summary: Paramonos says that he has already written about the thirty drachmae, asking Zenon either to send him a mattress or return the money, but now he would prefer to have the  mattress.  If it costs more than thirty drachmae, he will pay the difference to Ephermostos or any other person whom Zenon may name. He aks him also not to forget about the pig for the festival and to let him have one worthy of yourself and Epharmostos, Zenon and Epharmostos being pig-breeders.

title: Letter from Petosiris to Zenon
summary: A letter from Petosiris, informing Zenon that in accordance with his order he has measured out to Apollonios sixty artabs of wheat as rent for ten arourai of hay.  He asks Zenon to write to Leon requesting him to credit them with payment of this and also of ten artabs of lentils given to Theopompos for the pigeons.

title: Memorandum to Zenon from Dionysios
summary: Dionysios reports that he has gone to Koitai to guarantee payment of fifty drachmae on behalf of Ptolemaios, Zenon having ordered him to ask for five days in which to pay the money.  He requests Zenon to give his brother three mattocks to enable them to water the holding in good time.

title: Letter from Zenon (?) to Axates
summary: The writer reminds his correspondent about the priest of Thoeris in Philadelphia, to whom an allowance of twelve drachmae a year was due from the priest of Thoeris in some place not specified.

title: Receipts for Various Taxes
summary: Six receipts, all issued to Isidorus, son of Ptolemaeus, by various collectors and sitologoi.

title: Letter from Nikandros and Peisikles to Zenon
summary: Nikandros and Peisikles had already written to Zenon to thank him for having arranged about the money due to them, and they now ask him to collect as much of it as he thinks convenient before Diotimos sails up.  They ask him also to dispose of the house and the vineyard as soon as possible, in order that they may suffer no loss from this source either.

title: Letter from Artemidoros to Zenon
summary: A badly preserved letter from Artemidoros the physician, probably written in Alexandria.  He has heard that Petos the swineherd has run away and that a great many pigs are missing (the herd seems to have belonged to Artemidoros himself).  He therefore asks Zenon to have inquiries made as to where Petos has fled and to see that the securities are either jailed or bailed until satisfaction has been obtained. At the end he acknowledges receipt of a pig which Zenon has sent him.

title: Letter from Artemidoros to Zenon
summary: Artemidoros the physician writes from Alexandria about some honey which he had requested Zenon to buy for a friend of his called Apollonios living up the river at Ptolemais.  The honey was to be handed over to Ptolemaios, an agent of Artemidoros, who was to meet a certain Herakleodoros on his way up the river and deliver it to him.

title: Account of pigs
summary: In lines 1 - 8 it is stated that the swineherd Pnachthis has received so many young pigs and so many sows, amounting altogether to 226.  The following lines, 9 - 42, give a detailed list of twenty animals which have died or been killed, lost, or stolen.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter possibly about a robbery.

title: Letter from Aristandros to Zenon
summary: Aristandros reproaches Zenon for not having sent Herakleides the sitologos as he had promised and urges him to send him now without delay, as Agenor is making trouble and Aristandros is afraid of being brought to book.  He adds a sentence about the sesame accounts, saying he has inserted a note that most of the receipts submitted by Zenon come from people who already owed money for corn.

title: Letter from Pekysis to Zenon
summary: Pekysis writes tht Aristandros has ordered him to collect 1500 drachmae for the phorologos.  He had already talked to Zenon about a payment of 120 drachmae 1 obol 1 chalkous on behalf of the cleruchs whose land was being worked by Onnophris and he now s him to help to have this sum paid as part of the 1500 drachmae. He goes on to speak of the sesame, saying that Herakleides' people are unwilling to make the distribution without an order from Herakleides himself. Zenon should therefore tell Herakleides to write to the operators to measure out the sesame according to the apportionment proposed by Onnophris.

title: Letter from Pekysis to Zenon
summary: A fragmentary and badly preserved letter about a stathmos, about which Zenon had written in the belief that it was occupied by Philon.  Pekysis apparently replies that the peasants who used to live in it and who had gone away have now come back and reoccupied it.

title: Letter from Horos to Zenon
summary: Horos states that he has to measure up the works carried out in the last nine months and that he will need four papyrus rolls, three for the housebuilding account and one for the works of the vine-dressers.  He also speaks about his salary, which was at the rate of ten drachmae a month. Four months were due to him, but he had already received ten drachmae and owed fifteen; he therefore asks Zenon to let him have the balance of 15 drachmae.

title: Letter from Onnophris to Zenon
summary: Zenon had asked how much hay they had bought on the land of Andromachos, and Onnophris replies that they had grazed and reaped 12 3/8 arourai.

title: Loan contract
summary: From cartonnage. Cursive hand, same as BGU IV 1052, similar to BGU IV 1053, BGU IV 1055, BGU IV 1058.

title: Petition to a Beneficiarus
summary: A petition presented to Aurelius Leontius also called Isidorus, by Tha�sion and Kyrillous, daughters of Kopres, of the village of Karanis acting through their maternal uncle Ammonius.  Their father had died and they state that before his death their stepmother (not named) had admitted that her husband owed her nothing. Nevertheless, after his death, she induced her father to remove twenty-seven sheep from their father's flock. She then claimed that the deceased had owed her a mina in gold, but she failed to prove her claim. Finally she has brought into play a contract allegedly drawn up by Kopres which assigned her a half interest in a slave girl as security for her dowry. The plaintiffs in turn assert as law that dowries recorded in writing must be evaluated by a goldsmith (for jewelry) and a tailor (for cloth), and they deny that the contract of dowry or the contract of security will substantiate her demands.

title: Duplicate account
summary: A short account addressed to Pindaros and dated by the Macedonian calendar.  It gives a list of various articles belonging to various people, with prices attached.

title: Account of corn freight
summary: A short account dealing with the transport of corn from the Arsinoite  nome to Alexandria.

title: Account of oil
summary: Probably a household account, concerning oil.

title: Letter from Kriton to Moschion
summary: Demokrates the bearer of the letter had complained that Philokles, acting as toparch under Moschion, had  exacted fifty artabs of wheat from him, though he in fact owed nothing to the Crown, having bought the wheat from the harvesters.  Moschion to help the petitioner to recover his property and not allow his subordinates to take advantage of him, as he is one of Kriton's own men.

title: Copy of letter from Lysagoras to Diomedes
summary: Diomedes had apparently brought an action against Bion for a sum of 468 drachmae.  How far the proceedings had already gone is not clear, but in the present letter Lysagoras formally undertakes to produce Bion in Alexandria in the office or court of Apollonios or, if he fails to do so, to pay to Diomedes the said sum in the month of Audnaios, provided that he is not called away on Government business.

title: Fragment of a letter from Kallipos to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Kallipos to Zenon.

title: Agricultural account
summary: A list of cleruchs in various places, to whom the rest of the rent for year 36 is due to be paid.  The rent was paid partly in wheat and partly in barley, but sometimes the landlord preferred to have a portion of it commuted into money at the rate of seven artabs of wheat to the tetradrachm and about five artabs of barley to the drachma. The text shows what a large share Zenon took in the exploitation of the cleruchies.

title: Accounts
summary: A long account of money paid out for various purposes to various persons connected with Zenon.  Some of them are paid farmers, some cleruchs, some slaves and domestic servants.  Written on both the recto and verso.

title: List of silver vases held as security
summary: A long, confused and much corrected account of  money lent on silver plate and of the interest payable on the loans.  The vases were deposited in Philadelphia, Memphis and Alexandria, and various agents of Zenon or Apollonios are mentioned as taking part in the transactions, especially Theopompos, Krotos and Sosos.

title: Account of goats
summary: An account drawn up by Hermias, a goatherd.  He leased a large number of goats from Zenon, paying him a yearly rent of four obols and one kid for each goat.  The remaining kids became his own property, which he could dispose of as he pleased, and he sold for his own profit the milk and cheese obtained from the goats, and perhaps also the hair, which was used for making a coarse cloth. The balance in his favour is ten drachmae.

title: Petition to a Strategos
summary: A petition addressed to Aurelius Heron, strategos of the Arsinoite nome.  The petitioners are Ta�sis and Kyrillous, daughters of Kopres, of the village of Karanis.  They charge that their paternal uncle Chaeremon had appropriated all movable goods left by their father at his death. They appealed to the local prostates, but his decision in their favour had no effect on Chaeremon. They now therefore put their claim before the strategos and append a list of the property in question.

title: Letter from Apollonios and Menippos to Zenon
summary: Two vine-dressers ask Zenon to speak to Metrodoros about paying them for night-watching.  They have been to Bacchias inspecting and report that the new crop of grapes is promising well.  They also found that Atpheus had run away, owing to some trouble about the cultivation of certain vegetable plots

title: Letter from Pemnas to Zenon
summary: Pemnas, a swineherd, complains to Zenon that Herakleides, arranging privately with Thoteus, has made up the account of the arrears of rent owed by the swineherds, without allowing him to go through the account and check it.  He has already protested several times to Iason about their conduct.

title: Letter from Pemnas to Zenon
summary: Pemnas, a swineherd, is uneasy because he has not been allowed to see the accounts and suspects that Herakleides has entered to his debit a larger quantity than he really owes.  He also asks Zenon to send for the young pigs due to him as rent.

title: Letter from Korragos to Proxenos
summary: Korragos complains that in spite of an order given on the king's birthday he and his men have not yet received all the pay due to them.  He blames Leukippos the grammateus for this and asks Proxenos not to permit such conduct on the part of his subordinates.

title: Account of wheat
summary: An account of the wheat given out as food allowances to various members of Zenon's circle in the months of Pharmouthi and Pachon of year 38.

title: Account of sows
summary: An account from Herakleides of the sows owned by Zenon and leased to various persons.  On the verso is a list giving the number of sows which each of the swineherds actually has and the number of those which are missing.

title: Letter to [�.]odoros
summary: A letter about a woman, probably a paidisk�, who had run away with her child.  The writer desires his correspondent to see that, when arrested, she is not maltreated.

title: Letter from Dionysios to Zenon
summary: A fragment of a letter from Dionysios to Zenon.

title: Letter from Iason to Zenon
summary: Iason writes that he has gone to Moithymis to see Leon about the eparourion, of which five years' payment at the rate of three drachmae per aroura was being exacted for the vineyard and orchard.  He had asked Leon to wait and not sell the wine until Iason had written to Zenon. So Leon had given him three days in which to settle. Metrodoros also had written to Hermolaos to stop the sale until Zenon had been written to.

title: Fragment of a letter
summary: A fragment of a letter.

title: Petition to a Prefect of Egypt
summary: A petition submitted by Isidorus, son of Ptolemeaus, to Aelius Publius, preserved in two copies, concerning the case of arson described in Isid.65.

title: Fragment of a letter from Seos
summary: A fragment of a letter from Seos.

title: Duplicate contract
summary: After the usual protocol, the contract states tht Iason has leased out for two years to Demetrios and Limnaios 144 goats at an annual rent of 216 six-month-old kids.  The rent is to be paid half-yearly.  If they deliver less than the specified number of kids, they must pay at once a penalty price of ten drachmae per head; and at the end of the lease, if they fail to return the goats as they received them, they have to pay a certain sum for each one not accounted for. On the other hand, if Iason takes the herd from them before the agreed time, he must pay them a fine of one hundred drachmae. After the usual penalty clause authorising Iason to make exaction in case of default come the names of two sureties and six witnesses.

title: Drafts of a Petition to Apollonios from Theopropos,  a letter from Neon to Damonikos, and two memoranda
summary: Drafts: (a) A Petition to Apollonios from Theopropos, who calls himself a the�ros from Kalynda in Caria, stating that his farmer Theron had obtained from that city a contract for supplying the wine used at the annual festival in Kypranda.  Actually the wine was supplied on behalf of Theron by Theopropos himself, who had to borrow money to purchase it, and the sum due from the city for the supply of wine came to 850 drachmae. The city treasurers, Diophantos and Akrisios, had repaid 600 drachmae of this amount, but declined to pay the remaining 250 because the subscriptions had not all been paid up. Theopropos therefore asks Apollonios to write to the city and to the strategos and the oikonomos to see that the 250 are repaid. (b) A letter from Neon, another citizen of Kalynda, asking Damonikos to speak to Zenon on his behalf. Neon's father Therarchos, by an order from Alexandria, had been released from the obligation of providing quarters for soldiers or other employees of the State, as well as hay and pasture for the cavalry. When Therarchos died, the burden was reimposed upon Neon, who now asks for a new exemption to be written under his own name. (c) A draft of a memorandum on the same subject from Zenon, in which he mentions that Therarchos had married an aunt of his. He asks Apollonios to write again to Diodotos the oikonomos, the boule and the popular assembly, in order that Neon may enjoy the same privilege as his father. (d) A note about a letter to be written to a certain person, asking him to return a mare which he had borrowed, along with the little mule which she had foaled in the meantime.

title: Letter from Sisouchos to Zenon
summary: A repetition of a request to Zenon to write to the officials in Krokodilopolis asking them to give a clerkship to Ptolemaios, probably the author's son.  Sisouchos has sent the youth in person to see Zenon and begs him to write at once and get him appointed to a post with a fair salary.

title: Letter from Spinther to Zenon
summary: Some Government agents had made a descent on the boat on which Spinther was employed, demanding payment of the tax from year 39.  Zenon is therefore requested to write and say what arrangements Petosiris is to make.  Spinther adds that they have not received the pitch and that Rodippos was insisting that someone must render an account of this (?).

title: Letter
summary: The author, whose name is lost, asks his correspondent, probably Zenon, to write to Iason to let him have the lodging of Theopompos to live in.  His present quarters, he says, belong to Phileas the former secretary for the Arsinoite nome, who has now recovered his property from the dioiketes and is ordering him to evacuate. If the dwelling of Theopompos is not available, he asks for a letter from Sostratos telling his people to hand over his house.

title: Receipt in form of letter to Hermaphilos
summary: A receipt in the form of letter to Hermaphilos, the oikonomos.

title: Letter from Horos to Zenon
summary: An account in the form of a letter filing a list of young pigs received from various persons.  Zenon's brother Epharmostos take a prominent part in the business.

title: Deposition of a witness
summary: A witness states that, while he and Nikon and some others were in a barber's shop, Antipatros and Simon came and asked Nikon to given them back their son Theodosios, whereupon Nikon denied that he had even taken the boy.

title: Letter from Kallikrates to Zenon
